For example:\n\nReply from 93.184.216.34: bytes=32 time=180ms TTL=56\n\nThis tells us the server is reachable, the response took 180ms, and the packet passed through approximately 8 routers (64-56=8, assuming initial TTL of 64).", "explanation": "The ping command is a fundamental network diagnostic tool used to test connectivity, measure latency, and identify packet loss between two network nodes."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-047", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain two communication protocols used between devices in an Internet of Things (IoT) network.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) — a lightweight publish/subscribe messaging protocol designed for IoT devices with limited processing power and bandwidth. Devices publish messages to topics, and other devices subscribe to receive those messages through a central broker. It is ideal for sensor data collection (e.g., temperature sensors in a smart home). (2) Zigbee — a low-power, short-range wireless protocol designed for personal area networks. It uses a mesh network topology where devices can relay messages through each other, extending range. It is commonly used in smart home devices like lights, locks, and thermostats.", "explanation": "IoT communication protocols are designed for the constraints of IoT devices — limited power, processing, and bandwidth. MQTT, Zigbee, CoAP, Bluetooth Low Energy, and LoRaWAN serve different IoT communication needs."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-048", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "MC", "question": "What is a key benefit of interfacing machine learning (ML) with IoT?", "options": ["A) It makes IoT devices more expensive", "B) It enables predictive analytics and automated decision-making based on data patterns from connected sensors", "C) It slows down data processing", "D) It eliminates the need for any network infrastructure"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Combining ML with IoT enables devices to analyse patterns in sensor data, predict outcomes (e.g., predictive maintenance), and make automated decisions without human intervention, creating more intelligent and responsive systems."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-049", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A smart home network includes smart lights, a thermostat, security cameras, a smart lock, and a voice assistant. Describe two security measures that should be implemented to control access to this network and protect against unauthorised access.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Network segmentation — create a separate VLAN (Virtual Local Area Network) or subnet specifically for IoT devices, isolated from the main home network used for personal computers and sensitive data. This ensures that even if an IoT device is compromised, the attacker cannot easily access personal files or banking information on the main network. (2) Strong authentication and encryption — implement WPA3 encryption on the Wi-Fi network, use unique strong passwords for each IoT device, enable two-factor authentication (2FA) on the voice assistant and smart lock accounts, and regularly update firmware on all devices to patch known vulnerabilities.", "explanation": "Smart home network security requires multiple layers of protection including network segmentation, strong authentication, encryption, regular firmware updates, and monitoring for unusual activity."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-050", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a command that displays the current network configuration on a Windows computer, including IP address, subnet mask, and default gateway.", "options": [], "answer": "ipconfig /all\n\nThis command displays detailed network configuration for all network adapters on the computer, including:\n- IPv4 Address (e.g., 192.168.1.100)\n- Subnet Mask (e.g., 255.255.255.0)\n- Default Gateway (e.g., 192.168.1.1)\n- DNS Servers\n- DHCP status\n- MAC (Physical) Address\n\nThis information is essential for troubleshooting network connectivity issues and understanding how a device is configured within the network.", "explanation": "The ipconfig command (Windows) or ifconfig/ip addr (Linux/Mac) displays network configuration details essential for network administration and troubleshooting."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-051", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best describes data security measures for an intelligent home network?", "options": ["A) Using the same simple password for all devices", "B) Implementing firmware updates, encryption, strong passwords, and network segmentation", "C) Leaving the default settings on all devices unchanged", "D) Connecting all devices to a public Wi-Fi network"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Securing an intelligent home network requires a multi-layered approach: regular firmware updates to patch vulnerabilities, encryption (WPA3) for wireless communications, strong unique passwords, and network segmentation to isolate IoT devices."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-052", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "MC", "question": "When designing a network of interconnected devices, which of the following should be determined FIRST?", "options": ["A) The colour of the network cables", "B) The purpose and requirements of the network, including the number of devices, expected traffic, and security needs", "C) The brand of printer to connect", "D) The decorations for the server room"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Network design begins with requirements analysis — understanding the purpose of the network, the number and types of devices, expected traffic loads, security requirements, budget constraints, and scalability needs before selecting hardware and topology."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-053", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "TF", "question": "A Kanban board is a project management tool that visualises workflow stages such as 'To Do', 'In Progress', and 'Done'.", "options": [], "answer": "True. A Kanban board is a visual project management tool that displays tasks as cards moving through columns representing workflow stages (typically To Do, In Progress, and Done). It helps teams manage work in progress and identify bottlenecks.", "explanation": "Kanban boards are widely used in agile project management for developing network and interactive media projects. They promote transparency, limit work in progress, and support continuous workflow improvement."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-054", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a command to display the routing table on a Windows computer, which helps understand how data packets are directed through the network.", "options": [], "answer": "route print\n\nThis command displays the IP routing table on a Windows computer, showing:\n- Network Destination: the target network or host\n- Netmask: defines the network portion of the address\n- Gateway: the next hop router for reaching the destination\n- Interface: the local network interface used\n- Metric: the cost/priority of the route (lower is preferred)\n\nUnderstanding routing tables is essential for configuring devices within a network and troubleshooting connectivity issues between network segments.", "explanation": "The routing table determines how data packets are forwarded between networks. Understanding routing is fundamental to configuring and troubleshooting interconnected network devices."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-055", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe two security protocols that should be implemented when configuring devices within a network.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) WPA3 (Wi-Fi Protected Access 3) — the latest wireless security protocol that provides strong encryption for wireless network traffic. It uses SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) for more secure password-based authentication and provides forward secrecy, meaning past communications remain secure even if a password is later compromised. (2) HTTPS/TLS (Transport Layer Security) — encrypts data transmitted between devices and web servers, ensuring that sensitive information (login credentials, personal data) cannot be intercepted or read by attackers during transmission. All web-based network management interfaces should use HTTPS.", "explanation": "Security protocols protect data in transit and at rest. WPA3, TLS/SSL, IPsec, SSH, and 802.1X are commonly implemented protocols for securing network communications and device access."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-056", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is a method to optimise network performance?", "options": ["A) Adding more decorations to the server room", "B) Implementing Quality of Service (QoS) rules to prioritise critical network traffic", "C) Removing all security measures to increase speed", "D) Using the longest possible network cables"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Quality of Service (QoS) optimises network performance by prioritising critical traffic (e.g., video conferencing, VoIP) over less time-sensitive traffic (e.g., file downloads). Other optimisation methods include load balancing, caching, and upgrading bandwidth."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-057", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A small office is setting up a new network for 15 employees. They need reliable internet access, a shared printer, file storage, and secure Wi-Fi for visitors. Describe the key hardware and software components needed and explain how you would ensure security.", "options": [], "answer": "Hardware components: (1) A business-grade router with firewall capabilities to connect to the ISP and manage traffic. (2) A managed network switch (24-port) to connect wired devices. (3) One or two wireless access points for Wi-Fi coverage. (4) A network-attached storage (NAS) device for shared file storage with RAID redundancy. (5) A network printer. Software/configuration: (1) Configure the router's built-in firewall with appropriate rules. (2) Set up a separate guest Wi-Fi network (VLAN) for visitors, isolated from the internal network. (3) Implement WPA3 encryption on all wireless networks. (4) Configure DHCP for automatic IP assignment with static IPs for the printer and NAS. (5) Set up user accounts with appropriate file-sharing permissions on the NAS. (6) Install and configure antivirus software on all workstations. (7) Establish a regular backup schedule for the NAS.", "explanation": "Small office network design requires planning for connectivity, shared resources, security, and scalability. Hardware selection, network segmentation, and security configuration are all critical considerations."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-058", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following correctly describes the role of both hardware and software in data transmission?", "options": ["A) Hardware transmits data; software has no role", "B) Hardware provides the physical medium and devices for transmission, while software manages protocols, routing, and data formatting", "C) Software transmits data; hardware has no role", "D) Neither hardware nor software is needed for data transmission"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Data transmission requires both hardware (NICs, cables, routers, switches providing physical connectivity) and software (protocols like TCP/IP, routing algorithms, and network management tools that control how data is formatted, addressed, and delivered)."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-059", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best explains the concept of 'privacy' in the context of cybersecurity?", "options": ["A) The right of an organisation to share user data without consent", "B) The right of individuals to control how their personal information is collected, stored, used, and shared", "C) Keeping all data in a public database for transparency", "D) The speed at which data can be transmitted across a network"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Privacy in cybersecurity refers to the right of individuals to control their personal information — how it is collected, stored, used, and shared. This includes principles like informed consent, data minimisation, and the right to access and delete personal data."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-060", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain the difference between privacy and security in the context of data management. How do they relate to each other?", "options": [], "answer": "Privacy refers to the rights of individuals to control how their personal information is collected, used, and shared. It focuses on what data is gathered, who has access, and for what purpose. Security refers to the technical and procedural measures used to protect data from unauthorised access, theft, corruption, or loss. It focuses on how data is protected. They are related because security is a prerequisite for privacy — you cannot ensure data privacy without adequate security measures. However, a system can be secure without being private (e.g., a company may securely store data it collected without consent). Both are needed for responsible data management.", "explanation": "Privacy and security are distinct but complementary concepts. Privacy governs data usage rights, while security provides the mechanisms to enforce those rights through technical protections."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-061", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "TF", "question": "The Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) under the Privacy Act 1988 only apply to government agencies and do not affect businesses.", "options": [], "answer": "False. The Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) apply to Australian Government agencies AND private sector organisations with an annual turnover of more than $3 million, as well as some smaller organisations handling health information or trading in personal information.", "explanation": "The APPs regulate how both government and qualifying private sector organisations handle personal information, covering collection, use, disclosure, storage, access, and correction of data."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-062", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "MC", "question": "How can an individual best contribute to maintaining the privacy of their own data?", "options": ["A) By sharing passwords with trusted friends for safekeeping", "B) By using strong unique passwords, enabling two-factor authentication, reviewing privacy settings, and being cautious about what personal information they share online", "C) By posting all personal information publicly to demonstrate transparency", "D) By using the same password for all accounts to make them easier to remember"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Individuals can protect their data privacy by using strong unique passwords, enabling 2FA, regularly reviewing privacy settings on apps and social media, being cautious about sharing personal information online, and being aware of phishing attempts."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-063", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A popular social media platform experienced a data breach that exposed the personal information of 500,000 Australian users, including names, email addresses, and phone numbers. Describe two security vulnerabilities of social networking that this incident reveals, and explain how both individuals and the enterprise are affected.", "options": [], "answer": "Vulnerability 1: Centralised data storage — social networks store vast amounts of personal data in centralised databases, creating high-value targets for attackers. A single breach can expose millions of records simultaneously. Vulnerability 2: Inadequate data encryption — if the exposed data was stored in plain text or with weak encryption, it indicates insufficient security measures for protecting user data at rest. Impact on individuals: Exposed personal information can lead to identity theft, phishing attacks, spam, social engineering scams, and emotional distress. Users may lose trust in the platform and face ongoing privacy risks. Impact on the enterprise: The company faces regulatory penalties under the Privacy Act 1988 (mandatory data breach notification), reputational damage, loss of user trust, potential class-action lawsuits, and costs of remediation including credit monitoring services for affected users.", "explanation": "Social network breaches highlight vulnerabilities in centralised data storage, access controls, and encryption practices, with significant consequences for both individuals and the enterprise."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-064", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is an attribute of a cybersecurity breach?", "options": ["A) Improved system performance", "B) Unauthorised access to, disclosure of, or loss of data, potentially causing harm to individuals or organisations", "C) A scheduled system maintenance window", "D) A routine software update"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "A cybersecurity breach involves unauthorised access to, disclosure of, or loss of sensitive data. Key attributes include the attack vector used, data compromised, duration of the breach, number of affected parties, and the impact on confidentiality, integrity, or availability."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-065", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe two types of vulnerabilities that a threat actor might exploit in a cybersecurity breach.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Software vulnerabilities — unpatched software or operating systems contain known security flaws (CVEs) that threat actors can exploit. For example, an outdated web server with a known SQL injection vulnerability could allow an attacker to access the database and extract sensitive data. (2) Human vulnerabilities (social engineering) — threat actors exploit human psychology through phishing emails, pretexting, or baiting to trick employees into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links. For example, a spear-phishing email impersonating the CEO requesting an urgent wire transfer could lead to financial loss.", "explanation": "Cybersecurity vulnerabilities exist in software (bugs, misconfigurations), hardware (firmware flaws), networks (open ports, weak encryption), and people (social engineering susceptibility). Threat actors target the weakest link."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-066", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is a common cybercrime threat to an enterprise?", "options": ["A) Employees taking lunch breaks", "B) Ransomware attacks that encrypt company data and demand payment for decryption", "C) Regular office meetings", "D) Upgrading computer monitors"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Ransomware is a major cybercrime threat where malicious software encrypts an organisation's data and demands a ransom payment for the decryption key. Other common threats include phishing, DDoS attacks, insider threats, and data breaches."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-067", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "TF", "question": "A firewall is a hardware or software security measure that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined security rules.", "options": [], "answer": "True. A firewall acts as a barrier between trusted internal networks and untrusted external networks, examining traffic against security rules to allow or block data packets. Firewalls can be hardware devices, software applications, or a combination of both.", "explanation": "Firewalls are fundamental network security components that filter traffic based on rules, protecting internal networks from external threats while allowing legitimate communications."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-068", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe two hardware strategies and two software strategies used to protect data in an enterprise environment.", "options": [], "answer": "Hardware strategies: (1) Hardware firewalls — dedicated network appliances that filter all incoming and outgoing traffic at the network perimeter, providing a strong first line of defence against external threats. (2) Hardware security modules (HSMs) — physical devices that securely generate, store, and manage cryptographic keys used for encryption and digital signatures, providing tamper-resistant protection for sensitive data. Software strategies: (1) Antivirus and anti-malware software — continuously scans files, emails, and web traffic for known malicious signatures and suspicious behaviours, quarantining or removing threats before they can cause damage. (2) Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) — software that monitors network traffic patterns for anomalies and known attack signatures, alerting administrators to potential threats (IDS) or automatically blocking malicious traffic (IPS).", "explanation": "A defence-in-depth strategy combines multiple hardware and software security measures to create layered protection against various types of cyber threats."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-069", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "MC", "question": "What is the primary purpose of cyber risk management?", "options": ["A) To eliminate all possible cyber risks entirely", "B) To identify, assess, and prioritise cyber risks, then apply resources to minimise, monitor, and control the probability and impact of security incidents", "C) To install as much antivirus software as possible", "D) To avoid using technology altogether"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Cyber risk management is a systematic process of identifying potential threats, assessing their likelihood and impact, and implementing appropriate controls to reduce risk to an acceptable level. It acknowledges that risk cannot be entirely eliminated."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-070", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "An enterprise has identified the following cyber risks: (1) phishing emails targeting employees, (2) outdated server software with known vulnerabilities, and (3) lack of data backup procedures. Using a risk matrix approach, assess the likelihood and impact of each risk, and suggest a mitigation strategy for the highest-priority risk.", "options": [], "answer": "Risk assessment using a risk matrix:\n(1) Phishing emails — Likelihood: High (phishing is the most common attack vector). Impact: High (can lead to credential theft, ransomware, data breaches). Risk Rating: EXTREME.\n(2) Outdated server software — Likelihood: Medium (requires attackers to discover the specific vulnerability). Impact: High (could allow full system compromise). Risk Rating: HIGH.\n(3) Lack of data backup — Likelihood: Low (this is a vulnerability, not a threat itself, but becomes critical when combined with ransomware or hardware failure). Impact: Critical (total data loss with no recovery possible). Risk Rating: HIGH.\n\nHighest priority: Phishing emails (Extreme risk). Mitigation strategy: Implement a multi-layered approach including (a) mandatory security awareness training for all employees with simulated phishing tests, (b) email filtering and spam detection at the gateway, (c) multi-factor authentication (MFA) on all accounts to limit damage if credentials are compromised, and (d) clear incident reporting procedures so employees can quickly report suspicious emails.", "explanation": "Risk matrices help organisations prioritise cybersecurity investments by plotting likelihood against impact. The highest-rated risks receive the most urgent attention and resources."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-071", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best describes the impact of a cybersecurity breach on an individual?", "options": ["A) The individual always benefits from increased media attention", "B) The individual may experience identity theft, financial loss, emotional distress, and loss of privacy", "C) Cybersecurity breaches only affect organisations, not individuals", "D) Individuals are automatically compensated for any losses"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Cybersecurity breaches can significantly impact individuals through identity theft, unauthorised financial transactions, emotional distress, loss of personal privacy, reputational damage, and ongoing vulnerability to further attacks using the exposed data."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-072", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Identify two Australian laws or pieces of legislation associated with cybersecurity and briefly describe what each covers.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) — governs how personal information is collected, stored, used, and disclosed by government agencies and private organisations. It includes the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) and the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme, which requires organisations to notify affected individuals and the OAIC when a data breach is likely to cause serious harm. (2) Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth), Part 10.7 — contains offences related to computer crimes, including unauthorised access to or modification of restricted data, unauthorised impairment of electronic communication, and possession of data with intent to commit a computer offence. Penalties include imprisonment.", "explanation": "Australian cybersecurity legislation includes the Privacy Act 1988, Criminal Code Act 1995, Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979, and the Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-073", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "TF", "question": "Under Australian law, it is legal to access another person's computer system without their permission as long as no data is modified or stolen.", "options": [], "answer": "False. Under the Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth), unauthorised access to a computer system is illegal regardless of whether any data is modified or stolen. The act of accessing a restricted computer system without authorisation is itself an offence punishable by imprisonment.", "explanation": "Australian law criminalises unauthorised computer access itself, not just the consequences. Even 'looking around' without permission is a criminal offence under federal legislation."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-074", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is an emerging cybercrime threat to enterprises?", "options": ["A) Floppy disk viruses", "B) AI-powered deepfake attacks used for social engineering and fraud", "C) Dial-up modem hacking", "D) Y2K bugs"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "AI-powered deepfakes represent an emerging cybercrime threat, using artificial intelligence to create convincing fake audio or video of real people. These can be used for CEO fraud (deepfake voice calls requesting wire transfers), impersonation, and sophisticated social engineering attacks."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-075", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A hospital's computer system is hit by a ransomware attack. Patient records, appointment schedules, and medical imaging systems are all encrypted. The attackers demand a cryptocurrency payment. Describe the impacts of this breach on the individual (patients), the organisation (hospital), and society.", "options": [], "answer": "Impact on individuals (patients): Patients' sensitive medical records are compromised, potentially leading to identity theft and insurance fraud. Patients may face delayed or cancelled treatments, surgeries, and appointments, directly endangering health and wellbeing. The psychological impact of knowing personal health information may be exposed causes significant distress.\n\nImpact on the organisation (hospital): The hospital faces immediate operational paralysis — unable to access patient records, process admissions, or use diagnostic equipment. Financial costs include potential ransom payment, system restoration, legal fees, regulatory fines under the NDB scheme, and reputational damage. Staff must revert to manual (paper-based) processes, drastically reducing efficiency.\n\nImpact on society: Public trust in healthcare institutions and digital health systems is eroded. If the attack delays emergency care, lives could be at risk. The incident may trigger increased cybersecurity regulation for healthcare. It demonstrates the vulnerability of critical infrastructure, potentially encouraging copycat attacks on other hospitals and essential services.", "explanation": "Cybersecurity breaches in critical sectors like healthcare have cascading impacts across individuals, organisations, and society, highlighting the importance of robust cybersecurity in essential services."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-076", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Ubiquity of interactive media", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write an HTML anchor tag with appropriate attributes that would be used in a digital marketing email campaign to track click-through rates.", "options": [], "answer": "Shop Our Winter Sale\n\nThis link uses UTM (Urchin Tracking Module) parameters to track the effectiveness of digital marketing: utm_source identifies the traffic source (email), utm_medium identifies the marketing channel (newsletter), and utm_campaign identifies the specific campaign (winter_sale_2026). This data is captured by analytics tools like Google Analytics, allowing marketers to measure consumer engagement and conversion rates.", "explanation": "UTM parameters are a key digital marketing technique that enables tracking and analysing how interactive media campaigns influence consumer behaviour through measurable click-through and conversion data."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-077", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Capture, store and integrate data", "type": "MC", "question": "Which software tool is most appropriate for developing animated elements for an interactive media project?", "options": ["A) Microsoft Word", "B) Adobe Animate or Blender", "C) Windows Calculator", "D) File Explorer"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Adobe Animate is used for creating 2D animations, interactive content, and motion graphics for web and multimedia. Blender is used for 3D animation and modelling. Both are industry-standard tools for developing animated interactive media elements."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-078", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Capture, store and integrate data", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A photographer has 500 high-resolution RAW images that need to be prepared for both a print exhibition catalogue and an interactive web gallery. Explain the workflow for digitising and preparing these assets, including appropriate file formats for each purpose.", "options": [], "answer": "Workflow: (1) Import all RAW files into photo editing software (e.g., Adobe Lightroom or Capture One) for batch processing. (2) Perform colour correction, exposure adjustment, and cropping as needed. (3) For the print catalogue: Export as TIFF files at 300 DPI with lossless compression, maintaining full resolution and colour accuracy in CMYK colour space for professional printing. (4) For the web gallery: Export as JPEG files at 72 DPI with quality set to 80-85% (a balanced lossy compression that reduces file size significantly while maintaining acceptable visual quality). Also generate WebP versions for modern browsers, which achieve smaller file sizes than JPEG at equivalent quality. (5) Create thumbnail versions (e.g., 300px wide) for gallery preview pages to improve page load times. (6) Implement lazy loading on the web gallery so images only load as users scroll, optimising performance.", "explanation": "Asset preparation requires understanding the different requirements of print (high resolution, lossless, CMYK) versus web (optimised file size, RGB, responsive) and selecting appropriate formats and compression for each."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-079", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Capture, store and integrate data", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain three design tools or techniques that can be used to develop an engaging user interface.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Wireframing — creating low-fidelity skeletal layouts of a page or screen to establish the structure, content placement, and navigation flow before adding visual design. Tools like Figma, Balsamiq, or hand-drawn sketches help teams agree on layout early in the design process. (2) Colour theory and contrast — applying colour psychology (e.g., blue for trust, red for urgency) and ensuring sufficient contrast ratios (WCAG 2.1 recommends at least 4.5:1 for normal text) to create visually appealing and accessible interfaces. (3) Prototyping — building interactive mockups that simulate the final product's functionality, allowing designers to test user flows, gather feedback, and iterate before development begins. Tools like Figma, Adobe XD, and InVision enable clickable prototypes with transitions and animations.", "explanation": "UI design employs various tools and techniques including wireframing, prototyping, colour theory, typography, visual hierarchy, grid systems, and user testing to create engaging and effective interfaces."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-080", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Introduction to human-centric computing", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain how the development of hardware and software has influenced the adoption of the Internet of Things (IoT). Give two specific examples.", "options": [], "answer": "Hardware developments: Miniaturisation of sensors and processors (e.g., ARM-based microcontrollers) has made it possible to embed computing capabilities into everyday objects at low cost. For example, the ESP32 microcontroller costs under $5 and includes Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, enabling affordable smart devices like environmental sensors and smart plugs. Software developments: Cloud computing platforms (e.g., AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT) provide scalable infrastructure for managing millions of connected devices, while lightweight operating systems (e.g., FreeRTOS) enable complex functionality on devices with limited memory. For example, smart home platforms like Google Home use cloud-based AI to process voice commands and coordinate multiple IoT devices seamlessly.", "explanation": "The convergence of affordable miniaturised hardware, efficient software, cloud computing, and wireless connectivity has driven IoT adoption across consumer, industrial, and enterprise applications."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-081", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Introduction to human-centric computing", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best describes how web applications influence the success of social networking?", "options": ["A) Web applications have no impact on social networking", "B) Web applications provide cross-platform accessibility, enabling users to access social networks from any device with a browser, increasing reach and engagement", "C) Web applications only work on desktop computers", "D) Web applications make social networks slower"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Web applications enable social networks to be accessed from any device with a browser without requiring installation, increasing accessibility and user engagement. Combined with dedicated mobile apps, this multi-platform approach maximises the reach and success of social networking platforms."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-082", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Storage and workflow in enterprise networks", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a simple SQL query that could be used to investigate data storage requirements by finding the total size of files stored by each department in a company database.", "options": [], "answer": "SELECT department, \n COUNT(*) AS total_files, \n SUM(file_size_mb) AS total_storage_mb,\n ROUND(SUM(file_size_mb) / 1024, 2) AS total_storage_gb\nFROM company_files\nGROUP BY department\nORDER BY total_storage_mb DESC;\n\nThis query analyses data storage requirements by department, showing the number of files and total storage used. The results, ordered by storage consumption, help IT administrators understand which departments need the most storage resources and plan capacity accordingly.", "explanation": "SQL queries enable enterprises to analyse their data storage patterns, identify growth trends, and make informed decisions about storage infrastructure investments."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-083", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain three ways to improve data flow within a network system, considering proximity and modes of connectivity.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Strategic placement of network equipment — positioning switches, routers, and wireless access points closer to high-traffic areas reduces latency and signal degradation. For example, placing a server rack centrally in a building rather than at one end minimises the average cable length to all connected devices. (2) Using appropriate connectivity modes — implementing wired Ethernet (Cat6/Cat6a) connections for stationary devices that require high bandwidth and low latency (e.g., servers, desktop workstations), while using Wi-Fi 6 for mobile devices that need flexibility. This ensures each device uses the most efficient connection mode for its purpose. (3) Network segmentation with VLANs — dividing the network into virtual segments based on function (e.g., separating video streaming traffic from file server traffic) reduces congestion by containing broadcast domains and allowing traffic prioritisation through QoS policies.", "explanation": "Improving data flow requires considering physical proximity of devices to network equipment, selecting appropriate wired or wireless connectivity modes, and logically organising traffic through segmentation and QoS."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-084", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "TF", "question": "In graph theory, a 'node' in a computer network represents a connection between devices.", "options": [], "answer": "False. In graph theory applied to computer networks, a 'node' (or vertex) represents a device (such as a computer, router, or switch), while an 'edge' (or link) represents the connection between devices. Nodes are the entities; edges are the relationships between them.", "explanation": "Understanding graph theory terminology is essential for network design: nodes represent devices/endpoints, edges represent connections/links, and the arrangement of these elements forms the network topology."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-085", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "MC", "question": "Which IoT communication protocol is specifically designed for low-power, wide-area networks (LPWAN) suitable for devices that need to transmit small amounts of data over long distances?", "options": ["A) Ethernet", "B) LoRaWAN", "C) USB", "D) HDMI"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network) is an LPWAN protocol designed for IoT devices that need to send small data packets over distances of up to 15 km in rural areas. It operates on low power, enabling battery-powered sensors to function for years without replacement."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-086", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A manufacturing company wants to implement IoT sensors throughout their factory floor to monitor equipment temperature, vibration, and power consumption. They want to use machine learning to predict equipment failures before they occur. Describe how ML interfacing with IoT would work in this scenario and identify two benefits.", "options": [], "answer": "Implementation: IoT sensors attached to each machine continuously collect data on temperature, vibration levels, and power consumption. This data is transmitted via an MQTT protocol to a central server or cloud platform. The ML system is trained on historical data that includes normal operating patterns and patterns that preceded past equipment failures. The trained ML model analyses incoming sensor data in real time, comparing current readings against learned patterns. When the model detects patterns similar to pre-failure conditions (e.g., gradually increasing vibration combined with rising temperature), it generates an alert for maintenance staff before the actual failure occurs.\n\nBenefits: (1) Predictive maintenance — instead of following fixed maintenance schedules (which may be too frequent or too infrequent), ML-driven predictions allow maintenance to be performed exactly when needed, reducing downtime from unexpected failures by up to 50% and reducing maintenance costs. (2) Operational optimisation — ML can identify patterns in power consumption data that reveal inefficient operating conditions, enabling the company to optimise machine settings for energy efficiency and extend equipment lifespan.", "explanation": "ML combined with IoT enables intelligent systems that can predict failures, optimise processes, and make automated decisions based on patterns in sensor data, creating significant value in industrial settings."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-087", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a simple HTML form that demonstrates privacy principles by including a consent checkbox and a privacy policy link before collecting user data.", "options": [], "answer": "
\n \n \n\n \n \n\n
\n \n \n
\n\n \n
\n\nThis form demonstrates privacy principles by: (1) collecting only necessary data (data minimisation), (2) requiring explicit consent via a checkbox, (3) linking to a privacy policy for transparency, and (4) making consent mandatory (required attribute) before submission.", "explanation": "Privacy-respecting forms implement consent mechanisms, data minimisation, transparency through privacy policies, and explicit opt-in requirements — all aligned with Australian Privacy Principles."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-088", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following describes how enterprises use permissions to control data access?", "options": ["A) Giving all employees full access to all company data", "B) Implementing role-based access control (RBAC) where users only have access to data necessary for their role", "C) Storing all passwords in a shared spreadsheet", "D) Allowing anyone on the internet to access company databases"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Role-based access control (RBAC) is a security principle where users are granted permissions based on their organisational role. This implements the principle of least privilege — users only access data necessary for their job function, reducing the risk of unauthorised data exposure."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-089", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a configuration example for a basic firewall rule that blocks incoming traffic on a specific port, and explain when this would be used.", "options": [], "answer": "# Using iptables (Linux) to block incoming traffic on port 23 (Telnet)\niptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP\n\n# Using Windows Firewall via netsh\nnetsh advfirewall firewall add rule name=\"Block Telnet\" dir=in action=block protocol=tcp localport=23\n\nExplanation: Port 23 is used by Telnet, an unencrypted remote access protocol. Since Telnet transmits data (including passwords) in plain text, it is a security vulnerability. This firewall rule blocks all incoming TCP traffic on port 23, preventing anyone from connecting via Telnet. Administrators should use SSH (port 22) instead, which provides encrypted communications.\n\nThis is a common security hardening practice — closing unnecessary ports reduces the attack surface available to threat actors.", "explanation": "Firewall rules are fundamental security configurations that control network traffic by allowing or blocking connections on specific ports, helping protect systems from unauthorised access."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-090", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "MC", "question": "In a risk matrix, how is a risk typically classified when it has HIGH likelihood and HIGH impact?", "options": ["A) Low risk — acceptable", "B) Medium risk — monitor", "C) Extreme risk — immediate action required", "D) No risk — ignore"], "answer": "C", "explanation": "In a risk matrix, risks with both high likelihood and high impact are classified as extreme (or critical) risks that require immediate action, prioritised resource allocation, and implementation of strong mitigation controls."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-091", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Ubiquity of interactive media", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best demonstrates how interactive media supports creative processes in music production?", "options": ["A) Printing sheet music on a laser printer", "B) Using a digital audio workstation (DAW) with virtual instruments, effects, and collaborative features", "C) Playing a CD on a stereo system", "D) Recording audio on a cassette tape"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Digital audio workstations (DAWs) support creative processes by providing virtual instruments, effects processing, multi-track recording, and real-time collaboration features that enable musicians to experiment, iterate, and produce music digitally."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-092", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Ubiquity of interactive media", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A retail company uses targeted social media advertising, influencer partnerships, and email marketing automation to promote their products. Evaluate how these digital marketing techniques influence consumer behaviour, identifying one advantage and one ethical concern.", "options": [], "answer": "These digital marketing techniques influence consumer behaviour by: creating personalised shopping experiences through targeted ads based on browsing history and demographics, leveraging social proof through influencer endorsements that build trust and desire, and maintaining ongoing engagement through automated email sequences that nurture leads. Advantage: Consumers benefit from receiving more relevant product recommendations and discovering products aligned with their interests, rather than being exposed to random untargeted advertising. This can save time and improve the shopping experience. Ethical concern: The extensive tracking of user behaviour (browsing habits, purchase history, location data) raises significant privacy concerns. Consumers may not fully understand the extent of data collected about them, and the psychological manipulation techniques used in targeted advertising (e.g., creating artificial scarcity, exploiting FOMO) can lead to impulsive purchasing decisions, particularly affecting vulnerable groups such as young people.", "explanation": "Digital marketing techniques are powerful tools for influencing consumer behaviour, but they raise ethical questions about privacy, data collection, psychological manipulation, and transparency."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-093", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Create interactive media systems", "type": "TF", "question": "Responsive web design ensures that a website automatically adjusts its layout and content to fit different screen sizes and devices.", "options": [], "answer": "True. Responsive web design uses flexible grids, fluid images, and CSS media queries to automatically adapt a website's layout to the screen size and orientation of the device being used, providing an optimal viewing experience across desktops, tablets, and smartphones.", "explanation": "Responsive design is essential for modern web-based interactive media systems, ensuring accessibility and usability across the wide range of devices users employ to access content."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-094", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Create interactive media systems", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a CSS media query that demonstrates responsive design by changing the layout of a navigation menu from horizontal to vertical on mobile devices.", "options": [], "answer": "/* Desktop: horizontal navigation */\n.nav-menu {\n display: flex;\n flex-direction: row;\n gap: 20px;\n list-style: none;\n}\n\n/* Mobile: vertical navigation */\n@media (max-width: 768px) {\n .nav-menu {\n flex-direction: column;\n gap: 10px;\n }\n}\n\nThis media query applies the vertical layout when the viewport width is 768px or less (typical tablet/mobile breakpoint). The navigation items stack vertically for easier touch interaction on smaller screens, demonstrating the UX principle of adapting the interface to the user's context and device.", "explanation": "CSS media queries are a core technique for implementing responsive web design, allowing different styles to be applied based on device characteristics like screen width."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-095", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Storage and workflow in enterprise networks", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Compare public cloud, private cloud, and hybrid cloud in terms of access, services, and security.", "options": [], "answer": "Public cloud (e.g., AWS, Azure, Google Cloud): Access — available to any organisation or individual over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis. Services — offers a broad range of IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS options with virtually unlimited scalability. Security — the cloud provider manages infrastructure security, but the organisation shares responsibility for data security. Less control over data location.\n\nPrivate cloud: Access — exclusively available to a single organisation, either hosted on-premises or by a dedicated provider. Services — customised to the organisation's specific needs but with limited scalability compared to public cloud. Security — highest level of control over data, security policies, and compliance. Organisation manages all aspects of security.\n\nHybrid cloud: Access — combines public and private cloud, allowing data and applications to move between them. Services — offers flexibility to use public cloud for non-sensitive workloads and private cloud for sensitive data. Security — balanced approach where sensitive data remains in the secure private environment while leveraging public cloud benefits for other workloads.", "explanation": "Understanding cloud deployment models helps enterprises choose the right solution based on their security, compliance, scalability, and cost requirements."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-096", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is an example of device interoperability in an IoT-based enterprise network?", "options": ["A) A smart thermostat that can only be controlled by its own proprietary app", "B) Sensors from different manufacturers communicating through a common protocol to feed data into a centralised management system", "C) A printer that only works with one specific computer", "D) A device that cannot connect to any network"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Device interoperability means devices from different manufacturers can communicate and work together through common protocols and standards. In IoT enterprise networks, interoperability enables diverse sensors and systems to share data and coordinate actions through unified platforms."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-097", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe two procedures and two security protocols that should be considered when implementing cybersecurity for a new network.", "options": [], "answer": "Procedures: (1) Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) — a documented policy that defines how employees can and cannot use the organisation's network, devices, and data. It sets expectations for responsible use and outlines consequences for violations. (2) Incident Response Plan — a documented set of procedures that outlines how the organisation will detect, respond to, contain, eradicate, and recover from cybersecurity incidents. It includes roles, responsibilities, communication channels, and escalation procedures.\n\nSecurity protocols: (1) 802.1X (Network Access Control) — an IEEE standard that provides port-based authentication, requiring devices to authenticate before being granted access to the network. This prevents unauthorised devices from connecting. (2) IPsec (Internet Protocol Security) — a protocol suite that encrypts and authenticates IP packets, providing secure communication between network devices. Used commonly in VPN connections to protect data transmitted between remote offices or workers.", "explanation": "Implementing cybersecurity requires both procedural measures (policies, plans, training) and technical security protocols (authentication, encryption) working together to protect the network."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-098", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "CODE", "question": "Write a command to trace the route that data packets take from your computer to a destination server, and explain how this helps evaluate network performance.", "options": [], "answer": "tracert www.example.com\n\n(On Linux/Mac: traceroute www.example.com)\n\nThis command displays each router (hop) that data packets pass through on the way to the destination, along with the round-trip time for each hop. Example output:\n\n1 1ms 1ms 1ms 192.168.1.1 (local router)\n2 5ms 4ms 5ms 10.0.0.1 (ISP gateway)\n3 12ms 11ms 12ms 203.0.113.1 (regional router)\n4 25ms 24ms 26ms 93.184.216.34 (destination)\n\nThis helps evaluate network performance by: (1) identifying slow hops with high latency, (2) detecting packet loss at specific routers, (3) revealing inefficient routing paths, and (4) pinpointing where network bottlenecks or failures occur. Network administrators use this data to optimise routing and troubleshoot connectivity issues.", "explanation": "Traceroute is a network diagnostic tool that maps the path data takes through a network, helping identify performance bottlenecks, routing issues, and points of failure."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-099", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "An employee receives an email that appears to be from their company's IT department, asking them to click a link to 'verify their account' or risk losing access. The email looks legitimate. Describe what type of cybersecurity vulnerability this exploits, and explain three steps the employee should take.", "options": [], "answer": "This is a phishing attack — a form of social engineering that exploits human vulnerabilities rather than technical ones. It attempts to manipulate the employee through urgency ('risk losing access') and authority (impersonating the IT department) to trick them into revealing credentials or installing malware.\n\nThree steps the employee should take: (1) Do not click the link — hover over the link (without clicking) to check if the URL matches the company's legitimate domain. Phishing links often use similar-looking but different domains (e.g., company-secure.com instead of company.com). (2) Verify independently — contact the IT department directly using a known phone number or email address (not the contact details in the suspicious email) to confirm whether the request is legitimate. (3) Report the email — forward the suspicious email to the company's IT security team or designated reporting address (e.g., phishing@company.com) so they can investigate, warn other employees, and block the sender. Most email clients also have a 'Report phishing' option.", "explanation": "Phishing exploits human psychology (urgency, authority, fear) and is the most common initial attack vector in cybersecurity breaches. Employee awareness and proper reporting procedures are critical defences."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-100", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A hacktivist group claims responsibility for a distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack against a government website to protest proposed surveillance legislation. Discuss the ethical and legal dimensions of this action.", "options": [], "answer": "Legal dimension: Under Australian law (Criminal Code Act 1995, Part 10.7), a DDoS attack constitutes an offence of 'unauthorised impairment of electronic communication.' Regardless of the attackers' motivations, this is a criminal act punishable by imprisonment. The attackers could face charges under both federal cybercrime laws and potentially terrorism-related legislation depending on the severity and intent.\n\nEthical dimension: This raises a complex ethical debate. Arguments in support: The hacktivists view their action as a form of digital protest against surveillance they consider a violation of civil liberties, analogous to a physical protest blocking access to a building. Arguments against: The DDoS attack infringes on others' rights to access government services, may prevent citizens from accessing essential services, causes financial damage to taxpayers, and sets a dangerous precedent where any group can justify illegal actions based on their beliefs. The ethical principle that 'the ends do not justify the means' applies — there are legal channels for protest and advocacy that do not involve criminal activity.\n\nConclusion: While the ethical debate acknowledges the tension between activism and cybercrime, Australian law is clear that DDoS attacks are criminal offences regardless of motivation.", "explanation": "Cybercrime ethics involves complex discussions about digital rights, activism, and the law. Understanding both perspectives is important, but legal frameworks provide clear boundaries."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-101", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Ubiquity of interactive media", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe two ways social media applications encourage human connections, and evaluate one limitation.", "options": [], "answer": "Way 1: Real-time communication features — platforms like Facebook Messenger, WhatsApp, and Instagram Direct enable instant messaging, voice calls, and video calls that connect people regardless of geographical distance, maintaining relationships with friends and family worldwide. Way 2: Community and group features — platforms provide groups, forums, and interest-based communities (e.g., Reddit subreddits, Facebook Groups) that connect people with shared interests, hobbies, or experiences, fostering a sense of belonging and enabling collaboration. Limitation: Social media connections can be superficial — studies suggest that while users may have hundreds of online connections, the depth and quality of these relationships may not match face-to-face interactions. The curated nature of social media profiles can create unrealistic comparisons, and excessive use has been linked to increased feelings of loneliness, anxiety, and depression, particularly among young people.", "explanation": "Social media facilitates connections through communication tools and community features, but its impact on human wellbeing and the quality of relationships requires critical evaluation."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-102", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Ubiquity of interactive media", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is a legal issue when developing interactive media systems?", "options": ["A) Choosing an attractive colour scheme", "B) Copyright infringement when using unlicensed images, music, or code", "C) Deciding how many pages a website should have", "D) Selecting a font for headings"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Copyright infringement is a key legal issue in interactive media development. Using images, music, video, or code without proper licensing or permission violates copyright law. Developers must ensure all assets are properly licensed, attributed, or created originally."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-103", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Create interactive media systems", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best demonstrates applying features of user interaction within a web-based system?", "options": ["A) A static image with no clickable elements", "B) An interactive form with real-time validation, dropdown menus, and dynamic content loading based on user selections", "C) A plain text document", "D) A printed photograph"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Interactive forms with real-time validation, dropdown menus, and dynamic content loading demonstrate user interaction features. These elements respond to user input, provide immediate feedback, and adapt content based on selections, creating an engaging user experience."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-104", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Introduction to human-centric computing", "type": "TF", "question": "Dedicated mobile apps always provide a better user experience than web applications.", "options": [], "answer": "False. While dedicated mobile apps can offer deeper device integration (camera, GPS, push notifications) and potentially better performance, web applications offer advantages including cross-platform compatibility, no installation requirement, easier updates, and lower development costs. The best choice depends on the specific use case, target audience, and required features.", "explanation": "Both web apps and dedicated mobile apps have strengths and weaknesses. The success of social networking depends on offering the right combination of both to meet diverse user needs."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-105", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Storage and workflow in enterprise networks", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following is a benefit of using cloud computing services in an enterprise?", "options": ["A) Enterprises must purchase all hardware upfront with no flexibility", "B) Scalability — resources can be quickly increased or decreased based on demand without significant capital investment", "C) Data can only be accessed from one physical location", "D) Cloud services have no security features"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Scalability is a major benefit of cloud computing — enterprises can rapidly scale computing resources up during high demand and down during low demand, paying only for what they use, without large upfront infrastructure investments."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-106", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe three key components of an organisation's IT infrastructure and explain the role of each.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Servers — centralised computers that store data, run applications, and provide services to other devices on the network. They include file servers (storing shared documents), web servers (hosting websites), database servers (managing data), and mail servers (handling email). (2) Routers — network devices that direct data packets between different networks (e.g., between the internal network and the internet). They examine packet destination addresses and determine the most efficient path for data to travel, using routing tables and protocols. (3) Firewalls — security devices (hardware or software) positioned at network boundaries that inspect all incoming and outgoing traffic against security rules. They block unauthorised access attempts while allowing legitimate traffic to pass, forming the first line of defence against external threats.", "explanation": "IT infrastructure encompasses the hardware, software, networking, and security components that together support an organisation's technology needs and business operations."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-107", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Network architecture and infrastructure", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following best describes an edge in graph theory as applied to network design?", "options": ["A) A physical corner of a server rack", "B) A connection or link between two nodes (devices) in a network", "C) The boundary of a building where cables enter", "D) The border of a computer screen"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "In graph theory applied to network design, an edge represents a connection or link between two nodes. Edges can have weights (representing distance, cost, or bandwidth) and directionality, which help in optimising network topology and routing."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-108", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Creating a network", "type": "TF", "question": "Quality of Service (QoS) is a network feature that treats all data traffic with equal priority.", "options": [], "answer": "False. Quality of Service (QoS) does the opposite — it prioritises certain types of network traffic over others. For example, QoS can prioritise video conferencing and VoIP traffic (which requires low latency) over file downloads (which are less time-sensitive), ensuring consistent performance for critical applications.", "explanation": "QoS is a network optimisation technique that classifies and prioritises different types of traffic to ensure critical applications receive adequate bandwidth and low latency."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-109", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Understanding privacy and security", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe three ways an individual can contribute to maintaining the privacy of their own data in the digital environment.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Regular privacy audits — periodically reviewing privacy settings on social media, apps, and online accounts to ensure only necessary information is shared. This includes checking which apps have access to location, camera, contacts, and other sensitive data, and revoking permissions that are no longer needed. (2) Data minimisation practice — only providing the minimum personal information required when signing up for services or filling out forms. Using aliases or disposable email addresses for non-essential accounts and avoiding unnecessary sharing of personal details online. (3) Using privacy-enhancing tools — employing a password manager to create and store unique strong passwords for every account, using a VPN (Virtual Private Network) on public Wi-Fi to encrypt internet traffic, enabling two-factor authentication (2FA) on all important accounts, and using encrypted messaging apps for sensitive communications.", "explanation": "Individual responsibility is a crucial component of data privacy. While organisations must protect user data, individuals also have a role in managing their digital footprint and protecting their personal information."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-110", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe three different types of cybercrime threats that an enterprise might face.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) Ransomware — malicious software that encrypts an organisation's files and databases, rendering them inaccessible until a ransom is paid (usually in cryptocurrency). Modern ransomware attacks often include 'double extortion' where attackers also threaten to publish stolen data if the ransom is not paid. (2) Business Email Compromise (BEC) — a sophisticated scam where attackers impersonate a trusted figure (CEO, supplier, or partner) via email to trick employees into transferring funds or revealing sensitive information. BEC attacks cost organisations billions globally each year. (3) Supply chain attacks — attackers compromise a trusted third-party software vendor or service provider to gain access to their customers' networks. For example, inserting malicious code into a legitimate software update that is then distributed to all users of that software.", "explanation": "Enterprise cybercrime threats range from opportunistic attacks (phishing, ransomware) to sophisticated targeted operations (APTs, supply chain attacks), requiring comprehensive defence strategies."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-111", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Security awareness", "type": "TF", "question": "Encryption converts readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) to protect it from unauthorised access.", "options": [], "answer": "True. Encryption is a fundamental data protection strategy that uses mathematical algorithms to transform readable plaintext into unreadable ciphertext. Only authorised parties with the correct decryption key can convert the ciphertext back to readable form.", "explanation": "Encryption is a core software security strategy used to protect data at rest (stored data) and data in transit (data being transmitted across networks)."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-112", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "MC", "question": "Under the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme in Australia, when must an organisation notify affected individuals?", "options": ["A) Only if the breach makes headline news", "B) When a data breach is likely to result in serious harm to individuals whose personal information is involved", "C) Only if more than 1 million records are affected", "D) Notification is always optional"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Under the NDB scheme (part of the Privacy Act 1988), organisations must notify the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) and affected individuals when a data breach is likely to result in serious harm. This applies regardless of the number of records affected."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-113", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Principles of cybersecurity", "section": "Cyber law and ethics", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe two current or emerging cybercrime threats to enterprises and explain why they are difficult to defend against.", "options": [], "answer": "(1) AI-powered attacks — threat actors are using artificial intelligence to create more sophisticated phishing emails (grammatically perfect, highly personalised), generate deepfake audio/video for impersonation fraud, and develop malware that can adapt and evade detection. These are difficult to defend against because AI enables attacks to be highly convincing and automated at scale, overwhelming traditional pattern-based detection systems. (2) Zero-day exploits — attacks that target previously unknown software vulnerabilities before the vendor has released a patch. Threat actors discover and exploit these vulnerabilities in widely used software (operating systems, browsers, enterprise applications). They are difficult to defend against because by definition there is no patch or signature available at the time of attack, and traditional antivirus software cannot detect threats it has never seen before.", "explanation": "Emerging threats leverage advanced technologies (AI, quantum computing) and exploit the time gap between vulnerability discovery and patch deployment, requiring organisations to adopt adaptive, intelligence-driven security approaches."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-114", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Ubiquity of interactive media", "type": "MC", "question": "Which of the following hardware components would be LEAST important for a virtual reality interactive media project?", "options": ["A) High-performance GPU", "B) VR headset with motion tracking", "C) Dot matrix printer", "D) Fast processor with sufficient RAM"], "answer": "C", "explanation": "A dot matrix printer has no relevance to a VR interactive media project. VR requires a high-performance GPU for real-time 3D rendering, a VR headset with motion tracking for immersion, and a fast processor with sufficient RAM for smooth performance."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-115", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Interactive media and the user experience", "section": "Capture, store and integrate data", "type": "TF", "question": "SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) files use lossless compression and can be scaled to any size without quality loss.", "options": [], "answer": "True. SVG files are XML-based vector image files that define shapes using mathematical equations rather than pixels. They can be scaled infinitely without any quality loss and have small file sizes, making them ideal for logos, icons, and web graphics in interactive media projects.", "explanation": "SVG is a lossless vector format that maintains quality at any scale. Understanding the distinction between vector (SVG) and raster (JPEG, PNG) formats is important for selecting appropriate file formats."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-116", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Introduction to human-centric computing", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A new ride-sharing start-up is entering the Australian market to compete with established players. Using the concept of disruptive technology, explain what characteristics this start-up would need to succeed and identify two challenges it might face.", "options": [], "answer": "To succeed as a disruptive entrant, the start-up would need: (1) A unique value proposition — offering something established competitors do not, such as a subscription model for daily commuters, electric-only vehicle fleet for environmentally conscious users, or a cooperative model where drivers are co-owners. (2) Technology innovation — leveraging emerging technologies like AI-optimised routing, dynamic ride-sharing matching, or integration with public transport systems. (3) Customer-centric design — a superior UX in the app, transparent pricing, and excellent customer service. (4) Agile business model — ability to quickly adapt to market feedback and regulatory changes.\n\nChallenges: (1) Network effects barrier — established platforms already have large driver and rider networks. Users prefer platforms with more drivers (shorter wait times) and drivers prefer platforms with more riders (more income). Building this critical mass from scratch is extremely difficult. (2) Regulatory compliance — Australian transport regulations vary by state and territory, requiring the start-up to navigate different licensing requirements, safety standards, and insurance obligations across each jurisdiction, creating significant legal and operational complexity.", "explanation": "Understanding disruptive technology and start-up success factors helps analyse how new enterprises can enter established markets and the challenges they face."}, {"id": "EC-Y11-117", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Networking systems and social computing", "section": "Storage and workflow in enterprise networks", "type": "TF", "question": "Data backup is only necessary for enterprises that handle financial information.", "options": [], "answer": "False. All enterprises need data backup procedures regardless of their industry. Loss of any business data — customer records, emails, project files, configuration settings, or operational data — can cause significant disruption, financial loss, and compliance issues. Regular backups are a fundamental part of any organisation's data management strategy.", "explanation": "Data backup is a universal requirement for all enterprises.
