Failure to comply can result in significant penalties.", "explanation": "Legal compliance is mandatory for all Australian organisations handling personal data."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-103", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Creating intelligent systems", "type": "MC", "question": "In an expert system knowledge base, which format represents an IF-THEN rule?", "options": ["A) SELECT * FROM diagnoses", "B) IF temperature > 38 AND cough = true THEN recommend flu test", "C) for i in range(10): print(i)", "D) Hello"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "IF-THEN rules are the standard format for expert system knowledge bases, expressing conditional logic that maps conditions (antecedents) to conclusions (consequents)."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-104", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Creating intelligent systems", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain how you would implement automated processing using software for a smart irrigation system.", "answer": "Use Python or Arduino C++ to create event-driven software: 1) Read soil moisture sensors at regular intervals (e.g. every 15 minutes). 2) Compare readings against threshold values stored in a configuration file. 3) IF moisture < threshold THEN activate irrigation pump for a calculated duration based on deficit. 4) Log all readings and actions to a database for monitoring. 5) Implement scheduling logic to avoid watering during peak sun hours. 6) Add weather API integration to skip irrigation if rain is forecast. 7) Create a web dashboard for remote monitoring and manual override.", "explanation": "Tests implementing automated processing using software with real-world IoT application."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-105", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Enterprise project", "section": "Identifying and defining", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "You are documenting the development of a new enterprise CRM system. Describe the tools and processes you would use for project management and documentation.", "answer": "Tools: 1) Jira or Azure DevOps for task tracking, sprint planning and bug tracking. 2) Confluence or SharePoint for documentation (requirements, design specs, meeting minutes). 3) Git/GitHub for version control of code and configuration. 4) Lucidchart or draw.io for system architecture and data flow diagrams. 5) Slack/Teams for team communication. Processes: 1) Agile ceremonies (daily standups, sprint reviews, retrospectives). 2) Requirements gathering through stakeholder interviews and user stories. 3) Change management process with approval workflows. 4) Regular status reporting to stakeholders. 5) Document versioning and review cycles.", "explanation": "Tests knowledge of tools and processes for managing enterprise system development."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-106", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data science", "section": "Processing and presenting data", "type": "MC", "question": "What is the PRIMARY purpose of what-if modelling in a spreadsheet?", "options": ["A) To format cells with colours", "B) To explore how changing input variables affects outcomes", "C) To print the spreadsheet", "D) To password-protect the file"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "What-if modelling (via Goal Seek, Scenario Manager, Data Tables) allows users to change input values and observe the effect on calculated outputs, supporting exploratory analysis and planning."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-107", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data visualisation", "section": "Interpreting data visualisations", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "You are presented with a line graph showing Australian unemployment rates from 2015-2025. The y-axis starts at 3% rather than 0%. Explain how this design choice affects interpretation and what questions you would ask to interrogate this data.", "answer": "Starting the y-axis at 3% exaggerates visual differences between data points, making small changes appear dramatic. A drop from 5% to 4% would visually appear as a 50% decrease rather than a 20% decrease. Interrogation questions: 1) Why was the axis truncated - to mislead or to show meaningful variation? 2) What is the actual range of variation? 3) Are there seasonal adjustments applied? 4) Does the data source (ABS) use consistent methodology throughout? 5) Are there definitional changes (e.g. how unemployment is measured) that affect comparability? 6) What external events (COVID-19, policy changes) explain unusual patterns?", "explanation": "Tests critical interrogation of visualisation design choices and data interpretation."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-108", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Systems and their applications", "type": "MC", "question": "Which technology is MOST associated with current advances in AI-powered intelligent systems?", "options": ["A) Vacuum tubes", "B) Deep learning neural networks", "C) Punch cards", "D) Magnetic tape storage"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Deep learning neural networks (a subset of machine learning) drive current AI advances including image recognition, natural language processing and autonomous systems."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-109", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Data and intelligent systems", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "An Australian city council is considering implementing an AI-powered surveillance system with facial recognition in public spaces. Discuss the role of intelligent systems in this surveillance context, covering both benefits and ethical concerns.", "answer": "Benefits: Enhanced public safety through real-time identification of known offenders, missing persons located faster, traffic management optimisation, and automated incident detection reducing emergency response times. Ethical concerns: 1) Privacy - mass surveillance of citizens going about lawful activities. 2) Bias - facial recognition systems have demonstrated higher error rates for women and people of colour. 3) Function creep - systems deployed for safety may be repurposed for broader monitoring. 4) Legal compliance - must align with state surveillance legislation and the Privacy Act 1988. 5) Consent - citizens have not opted in to being tracked. 6) Data security - facial recognition databases are high-value targets for hackers.", "explanation": "Tests understanding of intelligent systems in surveillance with balanced analysis."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-110", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Enterprise project", "section": "Testing and evaluating", "type": "TF", "question": "User acceptance testing (UAT) is a form of validation rather than verification.", "answer": "True", "explanation": "UAT is validation because it checks whether the system meets user needs and business requirements (building the right product), rather than checking technical specifications (verification - building the product right)."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-111", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data science", "section": "Collecting, storing and analysing data", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain the difference between structured and unstructured datasets, giving two examples of each.", "answer": "Structured data has a predefined format and is organised in rows and columns (e.g. a relational database of customer records, a CSV file of sales transactions). Unstructured data has no predefined format or organisation (e.g. social media posts, medical imaging files). Structured data is easier to search, query and analyse using SQL; unstructured data requires specialised tools like NLP or computer vision for analysis.", "explanation": "Understanding data structure types is fundamental to choosing appropriate storage and analysis methods."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-112", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data visualisation", "section": "Using data to tell a story", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain how OLAP cube operations (drill-down, roll-up, slice, dice) support data analysis in enterprise decision-making.", "answer": "Drill-down: Moving from summary to detail (e.g. national sales to state-level to store-level). Roll-up: Aggregating data from detail to summary (e.g. daily to monthly to quarterly). Slice: Selecting a single dimension (e.g. viewing only Q3 data). Dice: Selecting multiple dimensions (e.g. Q3 data for NSW electronics). These operations allow managers to explore data at different granularities, supporting flexible ad-hoc analysis for strategic decisions.", "explanation": "OLAP operations are fundamental to interactive data analysis in enterprise environments."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-113", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Systems and their applications", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "A hospital implements an expert system for preliminary patient triage. Describe four key features this expert system would need and explain each.", "answer": "1) Knowledge base: Contains medical rules, symptom-condition mappings and triage protocols encoded by emergency medicine specialists. 2) Inference engine: Applies forward chaining to match patient symptoms against rules, deriving urgency classifications. 3) User interface: Simple, clear interface for triage nurses to input symptoms quickly; displays recommendations with confidence levels. 4) Explanation facility: Provides reasoning for each triage decision so medical staff can verify and override if needed, maintaining accountability and trust. Additional features: learning module to incorporate new medical evidence, and integration with the hospital patient management system.", "explanation": "Tests knowledge of expert system components in a practical application."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-114", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Enterprise project", "section": "Producing and implementing", "type": "MC", "question": "Which implementation approach runs the old and new systems simultaneously to reduce risk?", "options": ["A) Direct cutover", "B) Parallel running", "C) Phased implementation", "D) Pilot testing"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "Parallel running operates both old and new systems simultaneously, allowing comparison of outputs and providing a fallback. It is the safest but most resource-intensive approach."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-115", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data science", "section": "Collecting, storing and analysing data", "type": "TF", "question": "Ordinal data allows calculation of exact differences between values.", "answer": "False", "explanation": "Ordinal data shows rank order (e.g. 1st, 2nd, 3rd or Low, Medium, High) but the intervals between ranks are not necessarily equal. Only interval and ratio data allow meaningful calculation of differences."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-116", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data visualisation", "section": "Creating data visualisations", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Describe THREE methods to maintain data security when developing and deploying a data visualisation.", "answer": "1) Encryption: Encrypt data at rest in databases and in transit via HTTPS/TLS when visualisations are accessed via web. 2) Access control: Implement role-based access (RBAC) so users only see data they are authorised to view; use row-level security in tools like Power BI. 3) Audit logging: Track who accesses which visualisations and data, when, to detect unauthorised access and maintain accountability. Additional methods: data masking for sensitive fields, regular security audits and penetration testing.", "explanation": "Data security is critical throughout the visualisation lifecycle."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-117", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Systems and their applications", "type": "MC", "question": "Data flow diagrams (DFDs) are used in intelligent systems documentation to show:", "options": ["A) The physical layout of hardware", "B) How data moves through the system between processes, data stores and external entities", "C) The colour scheme of the user interface", "D) The company organisational chart"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "DFDs document the logical flow of data through a system, showing processes, data stores, data flows and external entities. They are essential for communicating intelligent system logic."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-118", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Enterprise project", "section": "Researching and planning", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain why selecting appropriate collaboration criteria is important for enterprise project success, with two examples.", "answer": "Appropriate collaboration criteria ensure team efficiency, quality and alignment. Example 1: Choosing Git for version control ensures developers can work simultaneously without code conflicts, with full change history for accountability. Example 2: Establishing regular sprint reviews ensures stakeholders provide timely feedback, preventing costly rework from misunderstood requirements. Poor collaboration criteria (e.g. sharing code via email) lead to version confusion, duplicated effort and communication breakdowns.", "explanation": "Collaboration criteria directly impact project delivery quality and team productivity."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-119", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data science", "section": "Processing and presenting data", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "Design a relational database for a small online bookshop. Identify the tables, key fields and relationships, and describe one appropriate user view.", "answer": "Tables: 1) Books (BookID PK, Title, Author, ISBN, Price, StockQty, CategoryID FK). 2) Customers (CustomerID PK, Name, Email, Address, Phone). 3) Orders (OrderID PK, CustomerID FK, OrderDate, TotalAmount, Status). 4) OrderItems (OrderItemID PK, OrderID FK, BookID FK, Quantity, LineTotal). 5) Categories (CategoryID PK, CategoryName). Relationships: Customers 1:M Orders, Orders 1:M OrderItems, Books 1:M OrderItems, Categories 1:M Books. User view for warehouse staff: A query joining Orders, OrderItems and Books filtered by Status='Pending' showing OrderID, BookTitle, Quantity and shelf location for order fulfilment.", "explanation": "Tests computational thinking in relational database design with user views."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-120", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Data and intelligent systems", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Explain how AI supports efficiency in an IoT network, with a specific example.", "answer": "AI analyses vast IoT data streams in real-time, identifying patterns and anomalies that humans cannot detect at scale. Example: In a smart building, AI processes data from thousands of sensors (occupancy, temperature, light, energy meters) to dynamically adjust HVAC, lighting and elevator scheduling. It learns occupancy patterns to pre-cool rooms before meetings, predicts equipment failures before they occur (predictive maintenance), and optimises energy consumption. This can reduce energy costs by 20-30% while improving occupant comfort.", "explanation": "AI transforms raw IoT data into intelligent automated responses."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-121", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data science", "section": "Collecting, storing and analysing data", "type": "MC", "question": "Which is an example of passive data collection?", "options": ["A) Conducting face-to-face interviews", "B) Distributing paper surveys", "C) Website cookies tracking browsing behaviour automatically", "D) Running a focus group"], "answer": "C", "explanation": "Website cookies automatically track user behaviour without active researcher intervention, making it passive collection. Interviews, surveys and focus groups all require active effort."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-122", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Creating intelligent systems", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "An expert system for car fault diagnosis has been producing incorrect results 30% of the time. Describe how you would assess the output by comparing proposed versus actual outputs and suggest improvements.", "answer": "Assessment: 1) Create a test dataset of known car faults with confirmed diagnoses. 2) Run each case through the expert system and record proposed diagnoses. 3) Compare proposed vs actual in a confusion matrix showing correct diagnoses, false positives and false negatives. 4) Graph accuracy rates by fault category using bar charts to identify which areas perform worst. 5) Calculate precision, recall and F1 score for each fault type. Improvements: Review and update IF-THEN rules for low-performing categories, adjust certainty factors based on observed accuracy, add new rules for fault patterns the system misses, and incorporate additional input variables (sensor data, mileage) to improve discrimination.", "explanation": "Tests systematic assessment of DSS output using graphing and comparison methods."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-123", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Data visualisation", "section": "Designing for user experience", "type": "SHORT", "question": "Discuss how emerging technologies like AR, VR and voice interfaces are impacting UI and UX design for data visualisations.", "answer": "AR overlays data onto the physical world (e.g. maintenance technicians seeing equipment sensor data through AR glasses). VR enables immersive 3D data exploration (e.g. walking through a virtual model of financial data). Voice interfaces allow hands-free querying of dashboards. These technologies require fundamentally different UX paradigms: spatial design instead of 2D layouts, gesture-based interaction instead of mouse clicks, and audio feedback instead of visual-only. Challenges include motion sickness in VR, processing latency, accessibility for users with disabilities, and the need for new design standards.", "explanation": "Emerging technologies are transforming how users interact with data visualisations."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-124", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Enterprise project", "section": "Producing and implementing", "type": "MC", "question": "A SWOT analysis identifies which of the following about an enterprise system?", "options": ["A) Only the financial costs", "B) Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities and Threats", "C) Only the technical specifications", "D) Only the user interface design"], "answer": "B", "explanation": "SWOT analysis is a strategic planning tool examining internal Strengths and Weaknesses plus external Opportunities and Threats, helping inform system requirements and limitations."}, {"id": "EC-Y12-125", "subject": "EC", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Intelligent systems", "section": "Systems and their applications", "type": "SCENARIO", "question": "Compare how a simplistic intelligent agent and a complex intelligent agent would handle the same search query: 'best Italian restaurant near me'. Describe the differences in their operation.", "answer": "Simplistic agent: Matches keywords 'Italian', 'restaurant' and 'near me' against indexed pages. Returns results based on keyword density and basic location matching. Does not consider user preferences or context beyond the literal query. Complex agent: Uses NLP to understand intent (dining recommendation, not just information). Considers user history (past restaurant visits, cuisine preferences, price range). Integrates real-time data (current location via GPS, restaurant opening hours, current wait times). Applies machine learning models trained on review sentiment, user behaviour patterns and collaborative filtering. Personalises results (e.g. prioritising highly-rated options matching the user's typical price range and dietary preferences). Uses predictive search to suggest related queries.", "explanation": "Tests understanding of simplistic versus complex intelligent agents in search engines."}]; const EXPLANATIONS=[{"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Software development", "content_text": "Explore fundamental software development steps used by programmers when designing software", "explanation": "## Fundamental Software Development Steps\n\nSoftware development follows the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**.\n\n### The Core Steps\n\n1. **Requirements gathering/Analysis** - Understanding what the software needs to do.\n2. **Design** - Planning: data structures, algorithms, UI wireframes, architecture.\n3. **Implementation (Coding)** - Writing source code.\n4. **Testing** - Unit tests, integration tests, user acceptance testing.\n5. **Deployment** - Releasing to end users.\n6. **Maintenance** - Ongoing fixes, updates, improvements (longest phase).\n\n### Key Terminology\n- **SDLC**: Software Development Life Cycle\n- **Stakeholder**: Anyone affected by or interested in the software\n- **Scope**: Boundaries of what the software will and won't do\n- **Iteration**: Repeating steps to refine the solution\n\n### Real-World Example\nBuilding a school attendance app: gather requirements, design database and screens, code the app, test with sample data, deploy to teachers, maintain.\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Ordering the steps correctly\n- Explaining why skipping steps causes problems\n- Identifying which step a described activity belongs to", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Analogy**: Compare to building a house - no bricks without a blueprint, no blueprint without knowing client wants.\n\n**Activity**: Brief for a simple app. Pairs write what to do at each SDLC step.\n\n**Misconception**: Students think coding is 90% of development. Requirements, design, and maintenance consume most effort.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Five scrambled SDLC descriptions - match to correct step and order."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Software development", "content_text": "Research and evaluate the prevalence and use of online code collaboration tools", "explanation": "## Online Code Collaboration Tools\n\n### Major Platforms\n- **GitHub** - Most widely used (100M+ developers). Repos, PRs, issues, CI/CD.\n- **GitLab** - Complete DevOps platform, popular self-hosted.\n- **Bitbucket** - Integrates with Jira/Trello.\n- **Replit** - Online IDE, good for education.\n- **VS Code Live Share** - Real-time collaborative editing.\n\n### Key Features\n1. **Version Control (Git)** - Tracks changes, enables reverting.\n2. **Branching** - Work in isolation.\n3. **Pull/Merge Requests** - Review before merging.\n4. **Code Review** - Team quality comments.\n5. **Issue Tracking** - Bugs, features, tasks.\n6. **CI/CD** - Auto test and deploy.\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Features supporting collaboration\n- Advantages/disadvantages\n- How version control prevents data loss", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Class GitHub repo. Each student creates branch, adds file, submits PR.\n\n**Misconception**: Git vs GitHub confusion. Git is the tool; GitHub is the service.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Three problems a 5-dev team faces without collaboration tools, and which feature solves each."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Apply computational thinking and algorithmic design by defining the key features of standard algorithms, including sequence, selection, iteration and identifying data that should be stored", "explanation": "## Computational Thinking & Standard Structures\n\n### Four Pillars\n1. **Decomposition** - Break problem into sub-problems\n2. **Pattern Recognition** - Identify similarities\n3. **Abstraction** - Focus on important details\n4. **Algorithm Design** - Step-by-step solutions\n\n### Three Structures\n- **Sequence** - Instructions in order\n- **Selection** - IF-THEN-ELSE decisions\n- **Iteration** - FOR (known count), WHILE (pre-test), REPEAT-UNTIL (post-test)\n\n### Data to Store\n- Input data, Processing variables (counters, totals), Output data, Constants\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Identify control structures in pseudocode\n- List variables needed\n- Trace algorithms", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Analogy**: Tea = sequence. Tea or coffee = selection. Stir until dissolved = iteration.\n\n**Activity**: Restaurant bill - identify data, structures, write pseudocode.\n\n**Misconception**: FOR vs WHILE confusion. FOR = known count; WHILE = condition-based.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Three algorithms - label structures, classify variables."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Apply divide and conquer and backtracking as algorithmic design strategies", "explanation": "## Divide and Conquer & Backtracking\n\n### Divide and Conquer\n1. Divide into sub-problems\n2. Conquer each\n3. Combine results\n\nExamples: Binary search O(log n), Merge sort O(n log n)\n\n### Backtracking\nExplore incrementally, abandon invalid paths, try alternatives.\n\nExamples: Maze solving, Sudoku, N-Queens\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- How D&C improves efficiency\n- Trace binary search\n- When backtracking is appropriate", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**D&C**: Number guessing 1-100. Middle guess finds answer in max 7 vs 100 linear.\n\n**Backtracking**: Maze on paper with pencil traces.\n\n**Misconception**: Backtracking seems inefficient but prunes early.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Trace binary search on 16-element sorted list."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Develop structured algorithms using pseudocode and flowcharts, including the use of subprograms", "explanation": "## Pseudocode, Flowcharts & Subprograms\n\n### Pseudocode\nKeywords CAPITALS, indentation, meaningful names.\n\n### Flowchart Symbols\nOval=Start/End, Rectangle=Process, Parallelogram=I/O, Diamond=Decision\n\n### Subprograms\n- Procedures: perform task, NO return value\n- Functions: perform task, RETURN value\n\nPromote code reuse, readability, maintainability.\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Convert between pseudocode and flowcharts\n- Write pseudocode with subprograms", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Sandwich recipe as pseudocode then flowchart with decision and iteration.\n\n**Misconception**: Pseudocode too vague or too language-specific.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Given flowchart, write pseudocode, identify subprogram candidates."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Use modelling tools including structure charts, abstraction and refinement diagrams to support top-down and bottom-up design", "explanation": "## Modelling Tools\n\n### Structure Charts\nHierarchical module breakdown with calling relationships.\n\n### Abstraction\nHigh level = what; Low level = how. Hide unnecessary complexity.\n\n### Refinement (Stepwise)\nLevel 0 general -> Level 1 sub-steps -> Level 2 detailed steps\n\n### Top-Down vs Bottom-Up\n- Top-down: decompose from overall to detail\n- Bottom-up: build from components upward\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Draw structure charts\n- Apply stepwise refinement\n- Compare approaches", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: ATM structure chart with decomposed modules.\n\n**Analogy**: Company org chart.\n\n**Misconception**: Abstraction means vague. It means focusing on what matters per level.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Complete Level 2 of a refinement diagram."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Analyse the logic and structure of written algorithms", "explanation": "## Analysing Algorithms\n\n### Logic: Correctness, completeness, termination, trace tables\n### Structure: Control structures, data flow, modularity, efficiency\n\n### Common Issues\nOff-by-one errors, missing ELSE, uninitialised variables, wrong operators, dead code\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Trace and determine output\n- Identify errors\n- Suggest improvements", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Three algorithms (correct, logic error, structural issue). Pairs trace and fix.\n\n**Misconception**: Mental tracing vs written trace tables. Written catches more errors.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 10-line algorithm with subtle error - trace table to find it."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Identify procedures and functions in an algorithm", "explanation": "## Procedures and Functions\n\n- **Procedures**: Perform task, NO return value\n- **Functions**: Perform task, RETURN value\n\n### Parameters\n- Value: copy passed\n- Reference: original passed\n\n### Identifying\n- PROCEDURE keyword = procedure\n- FUNCTION with RETURN = function\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Distinguish in code\n- Write each for tasks\n- Identify parameters and returns", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Analogy**: Procedure = 'turn off lights' (action). Function = 'what is 7x8?' (returns 56).\n\n**Activity**: Classify 10 tasks as procedure or function.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Four subprograms - classify, identify parameters and returns."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Designing algorithms", "content_text": "Experiment with object-oriented programming, imperative, logic and functional programming paradigms", "explanation": "## Programming Paradigms\n\n### Imperative: Step-by-step, change state. HOW to do it. (C, Python)\n### OOP: Objects with data+behaviour. Encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism. (Java, C#)\n### Functional: Mathematical functions. WHAT to compute. Pure, immutable. (Haskell, Lisp)\n### Logic: Facts and rules, derive conclusions. (Prolog)\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Compare paradigms\n- Identify paradigm from code\n- Best paradigm for a problem", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Same problem in imperative, functional, OOP style. Compare.\n\n**Misconception**: One paradigm is best. Most languages support multiple.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Four snippets - match to paradigm."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Data for software engineering", "content_text": "Investigate the use of number systems for computing purposes, including binary, decimal and hexadecimal", "explanation": "## Number Systems\n\n### Decimal (Base 10): 0-9, powers of 10\n### Binary (Base 2): 0-1, powers of 2. Computer native.\n### Hexadecimal (Base 16): 0-F, each digit = 4 bits. Compact binary.\n\n### Conversions\n- Decimal to Binary: divide by 2, read remainders\n- Binary to Hex: group 4 bits (10101100 = AC)\n- Hex to Decimal: multiply by powers of 16 (2F = 47)\n\n### Why Multiple?\n- Binary: how computers work\n- Hex: readable shorthand\n- Decimal: human-friendly", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Binary finger counting (10 fingers = count to 1023). Colour code decoding.\n\n**Misconception**: Memorise results instead of learning method.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 15 conversions across all three systems."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Data for software engineering", "content_text": "Represent integers using two's complement", "explanation": "## Two's Complement\n\nStandard method for positive and negative integers.\n\n### Positive: Same as binary. MSB=0.\n### Negative: 1) Write positive binary 2) Invert bits 3) Add 1\n\nExample: -5 = 00000101 -> 11111010 -> 11111011\n\n### Reading: If MSB=1: invert, add 1, negate.\n11110100 -> 00001011 -> 00001100 = 12 -> -12\n\n### Ranges: 8-bit: -128 to +127. n-bit: -2^(n-1) to +2^(n-1)-1", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Mnemonic**: Flip then Fix.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Start with -1, -2, -3 before larger values.\n\n**Misconception**: Forgetting to add 1 after inverting.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 5 decimal to two's complement, 5 back to decimal."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Data for software engineering", "content_text": "Investigate standard data types", "explanation": "## Standard Data Types\n\n| Type | Description | Example |\n|------|-----------|--------|\n| Integer | Whole numbers | 42 |\n| Float | Decimal numbers | 3.14 |\n| String | Text | \"Hello\" |\n| Boolean | True/False | True |\n| Character | Single char | 'A' |\n\n### Key Points\n- Type safety: wrong type causes errors\n- Type conversion: int(\"42\")\n- Static vs Dynamic typing", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Assign types to school enrolment form fields.\n\n**Demo**: Python \"5\"+3 (error) vs int(\"5\")+3 (8).\n\n**Misconception**: \"123\" is not an integer - it's a string.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 10 values - identify types."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Data for software engineering", "content_text": "Create data dictionaries as a tool to describe data and data types, structure data, and record relationships", "explanation": "## Data Dictionaries\n\nStructured reference documenting all data elements.\n\n### Per Field: name, type, size, description, constraints, default, relationships, examples\n\n### Why\n- Consistent data usage\n- Identify redundant/missing data\n- Support database design\n- Maintenance documentation", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Data dictionary for gaming profile.\n\n**Misconception**: Too vague (type: 'text'). Must be specific enough to implement.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Library system - 6-field dictionary with types, constraints, relationships."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Data for software engineering", "content_text": "Use data structures of arrays, records, trees and sequential files", "explanation": "## Data Structures\n\n### Arrays: Same-type elements, accessed by index\n### Records: Different-type fields grouped, one entity\n### Trees: Hierarchical nodes. Root, leaves, edges.\n### Sequential Files: Ordered data, read beginning to end\n\n### Choosing\n- Array: many same-type items\n- Record: one entity, multiple attributes\n- Tree: hierarchical relationships\n- Sequential file: persistent ordered storage", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Analogy**: Array = identical lockers. Record = wallet.\n\n**Activity**: Contact book with list of dictionaries.\n\n**Misconception**: Confusing array index with record field.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Music app - choose structures for each requirement."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Apply skills in computational thinking and programming to develop a software solution", "explanation": "## Developing Solutions\n\n### Process\n1. Understand (decomposition)\n2. Plan (abstraction, algorithms, data structures)\n3. Implement (code with good practices)\n4. Test and refine\n\n### Good Practices\nMeaningful names, comments, validation, consistent formatting, functions for modularity", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Project**: Full process: requirements -> pseudocode -> Python -> test.\n\n**Misconception**: Jump to coding. Require pseudocode first.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: New scenario - pseudocode with variables and types."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Implement data structures that support data storage", "explanation": "## Implementing Data Structures\n\n### Python Examples\n- Lists (arrays): `scores = [85, 92, 78]`\n- Dictionaries (records): `student = {\"name\": \"Alice\", \"age\": 16}`\n- Lists of dicts (array of records)\n- File I/O for persistent storage\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- Write code to create/manipulate structures\n- Choose right structure\n- File operations", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Hands-On**: Contact book with add, search, delete, save-to-file.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Arrays -> records -> combine -> file I/O.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Given data table, store in Python and search by name."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Compare the execution of the Waterfall and Agile project management models as applied to software development", "explanation": "## Waterfall vs Agile\n\n### Waterfall: Sequential phases, heavy docs, rigid, client at start/end\n### Agile: Iterative sprints, lighter docs, flexible, continuous client involvement\n\n| Aspect | Waterfall | Agile |\n|--------|-----------|-------|\n| Flexibility | Rigid | High |\n| Documentation | Heavy upfront | Lighter |\n| Delivery | One final | Incremental |\n| Risk | Late discovery | Early detection |", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Two teams build paper airplane - Waterfall vs Agile (3 sprints).\n\n**Misconception**: Agile = no planning. It plans continuously in smaller increments.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Recommend model for startup vs government project with justifications."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Test and evaluate solutions, considering key aspects including functionality, performance, readability of code, quality of documentation", "explanation": "## Testing and Evaluation\n\n### Four Criteria\n1. **Functionality**: Correct output, edge cases handled\n2. **Performance**: Speed, memory, scalability\n3. **Readability**: Names, formatting, comments, structure\n4. **Documentation**: User guide, technical docs, inline comments\n\n### Testing Types\nUnit, integration, system, user acceptance", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Two solutions to same problem - well-written vs messy. Evaluate both.\n\n**Misconception**: 'It works' is sufficient. Unreadable working code is a liability.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Evaluate own code against each criterion, suggest improvements."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Use debugging tools", "explanation": "## Debugging Tools\n\n- **Breakpoints**: Pause at specific lines to inspect variables\n- **Step-through**: Execute one line at a time (Into, Over, Out)\n- **Watch variables**: Monitor values in real time\n- **Call stack**: Chain of function calls\n- **Print debugging**: Quick but cluttered\n\n### Common Exam Angles\n- What each tool does\n- When to use each\n- Debugging strategies", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Demo**: VS Code debugger live on buggy program.\n\n**Misconception**: Rely on print statements only. Breakpoints are faster for complex bugs.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Match tool to scenario: wrong value (watch), wrong function (call stack), loop issue (step-through)."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Determine sets of suitable test data", "explanation": "## Suitable Test Data\n\n### Three Categories\n1. **Normal**: Typical expected inputs\n2. **Boundary**: Values at edges of valid ranges\n3. **Abnormal**: Inputs that should be rejected\n\n### Test Case Documentation\nTest ID, Input, Expected output, Actual output, Pass/Fail", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Percentage field (0-100) - generate complete set: 3 normal, 4 boundary, 3 abnormal.\n\n**Misconception**: Only testing normal data. Bugs hide at boundaries.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Password validator (8-20 chars, needs number) - 10 test cases all categories."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming fundamentals", "section": "Developing solutions with code", "content_text": "Determine typical errors experienced when developing code, including syntax, logic and runtime, and explain their likely causes", "explanation": "## Types of Errors\n\n### Syntax: Grammar violations. Detected before running. Missing colons, brackets.\n### Logic: Runs but wrong results. Detected in testing. Wrong operator, bad conditions.\n### Runtime: Crashes during execution. Division by zero, invalid index.\n\n| | Syntax | Logic | Runtime |\n|---|--------|-------|--------|\n| Detected | Before run | Testing | During run |\n| Crash? | Won't start | No | Yes |\n| Difficulty | Easiest | Hardest | Medium |", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: 9 snippets (3 per type) - classify, explain cause, propose fix.\n\n**Demo**: Deliberate errors showing different behaviours.\n\n**Misconception**: No error message = no errors. Logic errors are silent.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Given program and wrong output, identify error type and cause."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Apply the key features of an object-oriented programming (OOP) language", "explanation": "## Key OOP Features\n\n### Classes (blueprints) and Objects (instances)\n\n### Four Features\n1. **Encapsulation**: Bundle data+methods, restrict access\n2. **Inheritance**: New classes from existing ones\n3. **Polymorphism**: Same method, different responses\n4. **Abstraction**: Hide complexity, expose interfaces", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Analogy**: Class = cookie cutter; objects = cookies.\n\n**Activity**: School system: Person -> Student, Teacher subclasses.\n\n**Misconception**: Confusing classes with objects.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Identify which OOP feature each code line demonstrates."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Compare procedural programming with OOP", "explanation": "## Procedural vs OOP\n\n### Procedural: Functions operating on separate data. Top-down. C, Pascal.\n### OOP: Objects with data+behaviour bundled. Class hierarchies. Java, Python.\n\n| Aspect | Procedural | OOP |\n|--------|-----------|-----|\n| Organisation | Functions | Objects |\n| Data access | Global/passed | Encapsulated |\n| Reuse | Libraries | Inheritance |\n| Best for | Small programs | Large systems |", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Solve same problem both ways. See how procedural scatters data, OOP organises it.\n\n**Misconception**: OOP always better. For small scripts, procedural is simpler.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Two snippets - identify which is which, advantages of each."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Use data flow diagrams, structure charts and class diagrams to represent a system", "explanation": "## System Diagrams\n\n### DFD: Data movement. External entities, processes, data stores, flows.\n### Structure Charts: Hierarchical module breakdown.\n### Class Diagrams (UML): Class name | attributes | methods. Relationships: inheritance, association, composition. Visibility: +public, -private.", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Library system - draw all three diagram types.\n\n**Misconception**: DFDs vs flowcharts. DFDs show data movement; flowcharts show logic.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Create one of each diagram type from a description."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Describe the process of design used to develop code in an OOP language", "explanation": "## OOP Design Process\n\n1. Identify classes (nouns in requirements)\n2. Define attributes and methods (verbs = methods)\n3. Establish relationships (inheritance, association, composition)\n4. Create class diagrams\n5. Implement with encapsulation\n6. Write object interaction code\n7. Test: unit then integration", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Pet shop paragraph - underline nouns (classes) and verbs (methods).\n\n**Misconception**: Every noun becomes a class. Distinguish classes from attributes.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Pizza system - 3 classes with attributes, methods, relationships."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Assess the effectiveness of programming code developed to implement an algorithm", "explanation": "## Assessing Code Effectiveness\n\n### Criteria\n- Correctness, Efficiency (time/space complexity), Readability, Maintainability, Robustness, Reusability\n\n### Process\n1. Test with comprehensive data\n2. Profile performance\n3. Code review\n4. Evaluate structure", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Two solutions - elegant vs messy. Rate on criteria 1-5.\n\n**Misconception**: Works = good. Show O(n^2) failing on large data.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Evaluate own code, suggest one improvement per criterion."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Investigate how OOP languages handle message-passing between objects", "explanation": "## Message Passing\n\nObjects communicate by invoking methods on each other.\n\n- **Sender**: Object calling the method\n- **Receiver**: Object whose method is called\n- **Message**: Method name + parameters\n- **Response**: Return value\n\n### Benefits\n- Interact through defined interfaces\n- Encapsulation maintained\n- Loose coupling\n- Polymorphism enabled", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Analogy**: Sending a letter - write message, address to someone, they process and reply.\n\n**Activity**: Restaurant model: Customer -> Waiter -> Chef messages.\n\n**Misconception**: Direct attribute access instead of methods.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 3 classes - describe 3 messages between them."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Explain code optimisation in software engineering", "explanation": "## Code Optimisation\n\n### Performance: Efficient algorithms, appropriate data structures, minimise I/O\n### Memory: Appropriate types, lazy loading, generators\n### Readability: Refactor, remove dead code, design patterns\n\n### Best Practices\n1. Write correct readable code FIRST\n2. Profile to find bottlenecks\n3. Optimise only bottlenecks\n4. Measure improvement\n5. Don't sacrifice readability for minor gains", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Optimise inefficient code step by step, measure time difference.\n\n**Misconception**: Optimise everything. 80/20 rule: find the 20% causing 80% of slowdown.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Code with three inefficiencies - identify and suggest improvements."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Understanding OOP", "content_text": "Outline the features of OOP that support collaborative code development", "explanation": "## OOP for Collaboration\n\n- **Encapsulation**: Work on classes independently with agreed interfaces\n- **Inheritance**: Shared base classes, independent subclasses\n- **Polymorphism**: Add new types without modifying existing code\n- **Interfaces**: Public methods as contracts between developers\n- **Modularity**: Classes/packages owned by different members", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Team project - each student builds a different class. Agree interfaces, code independently, integrate.\n\n**Misconception**: Collaboration = sharing files. True OOP collaboration = interface contracts.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Three OOP features and how each helps a 4-person team."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Programming in OOP", "content_text": "Design and implement computer programs involving branching, iteration and functions in an OOP language for an identified need or opportunity", "explanation": "## OOP with Control Structures\n\nOOP uses the same structures (branching, iteration, functions) organised within classes.\n\n- Branching: IF/ELSE inside methods\n- Iteration: FOR/WHILE to process collections\n- Functions: Methods encapsulating behaviour\n\n### Design Process\n1. Identify need\n2. Class diagrams\n3. Implement\n4. Test methods then interactions", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Project**: Real need (quiz app, inventory). OOP solution with 2+ classes.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide class diagram, students implement method bodies.\n\n**Misconception**: All logic in one class. Distribute across appropriate classes.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Implement two methods - one branching, one iteration."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Programming in OOP", "content_text": "Implement and modify OOP programming code", "explanation": "## Implementing and Modifying OOP Code\n\n### Implementation\n- Creating classes with constructors\n- Inheritance with super()\n- Method overriding\n- Encapsulation (private/public)\n\n### Modification\n- Adding methods, overriding, refactoring, extending with subclasses, bug fixing\n\n### Good Practices\n- Short focused methods, meaningful names, maintain encapsulation, consistent style, docstrings", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Given base class, add method, create subclass, override method.\n\n**Pair Programming**: One writes class, partner modifies.\n\n**Misconception**: Duplicate code instead of inheritance.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Base Vehicle -> ElectricVehicle subclass with new attribute and overridden method."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "The object-oriented paradigm", "section": "Programming in OOP", "content_text": "Apply methodologies to test and evaluate code", "explanation": "## Testing OOP Code\n\n- **Unit Testing**: Individual methods/classes in isolation\n- **Integration Testing**: Object interactions\n- **Regression Testing**: Re-run after modifications\n- **Code Review**: OOP principles, readability, documentation\n- **TDD**: Write failing test, minimal code to pass, refactor", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Write unit tests BEFORE seeing implementation.\n\n**Code Review**: Pairs swap and review against checklist.\n\n**Misconception**: Only testing happy path. Require edge case and error tests.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Class with 4 methods - 2 unit tests per method."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Outline applications of mechatronic systems in a variety of specialised fields", "explanation": "## Mechatronic Applications\n\n- **Healthcare**: Robotic surgery, prosthetics, automated dispensing\n- **Manufacturing**: Assembly lines, CNC, quality control\n- **Agriculture**: Autonomous tractors, drones, automated irrigation\n- **Transport**: Autonomous vehicles, ABS, drone delivery\n- **Space/Defence**: Mars rovers, satellites, military drones\n- **Consumer**: Robot vacuums, smart home, gaming controllers", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Research one system - present sensors, actuators, software control.\n\n**Misconception**: Mechatronics = robots only. ABS brakes and elevators are mechatronic.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Three fields with specific application, sensor, and actuator each."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Identify the hardware requirements to run a program and the effect on code development", "explanation": "## Hardware Requirements\n\n### Key Hardware: Processor (speed), RAM (data capacity), I/O Pins, Communication interfaces\n\n### Effects on Code\n- Limited processor: efficient algorithms needed\n- Limited RAM: compact types, streaming\n- Real-time: millisecond response constraints\n- Power: energy-efficient code, sleep modes", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Compare Arduino (2KB RAM) vs Raspberry Pi (8GB). What can each run?\n\n**Misconception**: All computers equally powerful.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Given system requirement, identify hardware and code adjustments."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Identify and describe a range of sensors, actuators and end effectors/manipulators within existing mechatronic systems", "explanation": "## Sensors, Actuators, End Effectors\n\n### Sensors (Input): Ultrasonic, IR, Temperature, Light, Accelerometer, Pressure, Colour, GPS\n### Actuators (Output): DC Motor, Servo, Stepper, Solenoid, LED, Buzzer, Relay\n### End Effectors: Grippers, Welding torch, Spray nozzle, Suction cup, Cutting tool", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Hands-On**: Real sensors and actuators if possible.\n\n**Rule**: Sensors SENSE (input), Actuators ACT (output).\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Robotic arm design - 2 sensors, 2 actuators, 1 end effector with justifications."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Use different types of data and understand how it is obtained and processed in a mechatronic system", "explanation": "## Data in Mechatronics\n\n### Types: Analogue (continuous) vs Digital (discrete 0/1)\n\n### Obtaining: Sensor reads -> ADC converts analogue to digital -> microcontroller processes\n\n### Processing Pipeline\n1. Raw read (noisy)\n2. Filter/smooth\n3. Convert (calibration formula)\n4. Compare to thresholds\n5. Control decision\n6. Actuator output", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Demo**: Temperature sensor -> raw ADC -> voltage -> Celsius. Live pipeline.\n\n**Misconception**: Sensors give ready values. Raw data needs conversion and filtering.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Given raw ADC and specs, calculate physical measurement."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Experiment with software to control interactions and dependencies within mechatronic systems", "explanation": "## Software Control\n\nSoftware coordinates hardware: reads sensors, applies logic, commands actuators, manages timing.\n\n### Dependencies: Motor waits for safety sensor. Multiple actuators coordinate.\n\n### Approaches\n- Sequential: steps in order\n- Event-driven: respond to events\n- State machine: defined states and transitions\n\n### Tools: Arduino IDE, MicroPython, Tinkercad, Wokwi", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Tinkercad traffic light with dependencies. Add pedestrian button.\n\n**Misconception**: Code not handling dependencies between components.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: State diagram for robot with states and sensor triggers."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Determine power, battery and material requirements for components of a mechatronic system", "explanation": "## Power and Materials\n\n### Power: P = V x I. Total = sum of all components.\n### Battery: Capacity (mAh), voltage, discharge rate, weight, safety\n### Materials: Chassis, wiring gauge, connectors, mounting, environmental protection\n\nExample: Arduino(50mA) + 2 servos(400mA) + sensor(15mA) = 465mA. 2hr = 930mAh minimum.", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Plan mobile robot. Look up datasheets, calculate power, select battery.\n\n**Misconception**: Forget motors draw more under load.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Component list + operating time -> select battery with justification."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Develop wiring diagrams for a mechatronic system", "explanation": "## Wiring Diagrams\n\nVisual representation of electronic connections using standardised symbols.\n\n### Elements: Power rails, signal lines, pin assignments, component values\n\n### Best Practices\n- Label everything\n- Show pin numbers\n- Colour code (red=power, black=ground)\n- Include protection components\n\n### Tools: Fritzing, Tinkercad, KiCad", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Start simple (LED+resistor). Add complexity incrementally.\n\n**Misconception**: Forget ground connections or protection components.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Draw wiring diagram for LED controlled by light sensor via Arduino."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Understanding mechatronic hardware and software", "content_text": "Determine specialist requirements that influence the design and functions of mechatronic systems designed for people with disability", "explanation": "## Designing for Disability\n\n### Requirements\n- **Accessibility**: Multiple input methods, adjustable sensitivity\n- **Safety**: Force-limiting, emergency stop, fail-safe\n- **Customisability**: Programmable per user\n- **Reliability**: Daily dependence\n- **Ergonomics**: Comfortable, compatible with equipment\n- **Feedback**: Visual + audio + haptic for different abilities", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Empathy Activity**: Simulate disabilities, try standard interfaces.\n\n**Design Challenge**: Mechatronic solution for specific accessibility scenario.\n\n**Misconception**: Design for one disability only. Solutions should be customisable.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Limited hand mobility - 5 requirements and design influence."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Designing control algorithms", "content_text": "Develop, modify and apply algorithms to control a mechatronic system", "explanation": "## Control Algorithms\n\n### Structure: INITIALISE -> LOOP: READ, PROCESS, SEND, WAIT\n\n### Types\n- On/Off: compare to threshold\n- Proportional: output proportional to error\n- PID: P+I+D for optimal control\n\n### Modifying: Tune parameters, add safety, add hysteresis, optimise timing", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Temperature control progression: on/off -> hysteresis -> proportional.\n\n**Misconception**: On/off is good enough. Show oscillation.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Line-following robot algorithm. Modify for lost-line recovery."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Designing control algorithms", "content_text": "Explore the algorithmic patterns, code and applications for open and closed control systems", "explanation": "## Open vs Closed Loop\n\n### Open: No feedback. Execute regardless of result. Simple but inaccurate.\nExamples: Toaster, washing machine timer.\n\n### Closed: Sensor feedback adjusts output continuously. Complex but accurate.\nExamples: Thermostat, cruise control, self-balancing robot.\n\n### Applications\n- Open: predictable conditions, precision not critical\n- Closed: accuracy matters, conditions vary, safety important", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Demo**: Robot drives 3sec (open) vs drives until 20cm from wall (closed).\n\n**Misconception**: All systems should be closed. Some work fine open-loop.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Given open-loop algorithm, add sensor for closed-loop."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Designing control algorithms", "content_text": "Outline the features of an algorithm and program code used for autonomous control", "explanation": "## Autonomous Control\n\nOperating independently without human intervention.\n\n### Features\n- Continuous sensing (multiple sensors)\n- Decision-making (rules, state machines, ML)\n- Self-correction\n- Goal-oriented\n- Safety systems (failsafes)\n\n### Algorithm: INITIALISE -> WHILE goal NOT reached: SENSE, ASSESS, PLAN, EXECUTE, VERIFY, CHECK safety", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Program autonomous robot: explore, avoid obstacles, return.\n\n**Misconception**: Autonomous = perfect. All systems have limitations.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 5 features an autonomous delivery drone needs."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Design, develop and produce a mechatronic system for a real-world problem", "explanation": "## Building Mechatronic Systems\n\n### Process: Identify problem -> Research -> Requirements -> Design -> Prototype -> Test -> Document\n\n### Considerations\n- User needs and context\n- Constraints (budget, size, power, safety)\n- Feasibility\n- Integration of mechanical, electronic, software", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Project**: Real problem (plant watering, security alarm). Full design-build-test.\n\n**Misconception**: Skip planning, jump to building. Require documentation first.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Write requirements: 5 functional, 3 non-functional."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Implement algorithms and design programming code to drive mechatronic devices", "explanation": "## Programming Mechatronic Devices\n\n### Core Patterns: Sensor reading, motor control (PWM), main control loop\n\n### Best Practices\n- Functions per component\n- Error handling for hardware\n- Appropriate delays\n- PWM for smooth control\n- Safety timeouts\n- Hardware-specific comments", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Incremental**: Blink LED -> read button -> LED by button -> motor by sensor.\n\n**Misconception**: Blocking code (long delays) prevents sensor reads.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Code for distance sensor controlling a servo."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Develop simulations and prototypes of a potential mechatronic system to test programming code", "explanation": "## Simulations and Prototypes\n\n### Simulations: Software models. No cost, safe, fast. May miss real-world variables.\n### Prototypes: Physical hardware. Tests real physics. More expensive, slower.\n\n### Workflow: Simulate -> breadboard components -> integrated prototype -> refined -> final build", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Design in Tinkercad, build on real breadboard. Compare.\n\n**Misconception**: Simulation success = real success. Real testing is essential.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 3 advantages simulation, 3 advantages prototyping. Why need both."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement programming code for a closed loop control system", "explanation": "## Closed-Loop Implementation\n\n### Structure: SET setpoint -> LOOP: read, calculate error, control response, apply, wait\n\n### Design Decisions\n- Sampling rate (too fast = noise, too slow = poor response)\n- Control algorithm (on/off, proportional, PID)\n- Output limits (clamp to safe range)\n- Hysteresis\n- Error handling", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Temperature control: on/off -> hysteresis -> proportional. Graph each.\n\n**Misconception**: Sampling rate too high or low.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Closed-loop water level control with sensor disconnect handling."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Apply programming code to integrate sensors, actuators and end effectors/manipulators", "explanation": "## Integrating Components\n\n### Pattern: Initialise all -> LOOP: read sensors, apply logic, coordinate actuators/effectors\n\n### Challenges: Timing, priority, coordination, calibration, electrical noise\n\n### Best Practices: Abstract into functions/classes, consistent units, test individually first, logging, graceful degradation", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Project**: Pick-and-place: ultrasonic + motor + servo gripper.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Test individually -> combine two -> all three.\n\n**Misconception**: Integrate everything at once.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: 2 sensors + 1 motor + 1 servo - integration pseudocode."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Implement specific control algorithms that enhance the performance of a mechatronic system", "explanation": "## Advanced Control\n\n### PID Controller\n- P (Proportional): response to current error\n- I (Integral): accumulated past error (eliminates offset)\n- D (Derivative): rate of change (dampens oscillation)\n\n### Tuning: P only until oscillation -> add D -> add I\n\n### Other: Moving average filter, fuzzy logic", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Visual**: Graph P-only, PD, PID control responses.\n\n**Activity**: PID for line-following robot. Tune parameters.\n\n**Misconception**: Complex always better. Sometimes on/off suffices.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Effects of Kp too high, Ki too high, Kd too high."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement a user interface (UI) to control a mechatronic system", "explanation": "## Mechatronic UIs\n\n### Types: Physical (buttons/LEDs), Screen, Web-based, Mobile app\n\n### Elements: Controls (start/stop, sliders), Displays (readings, status), Feedback (visual/audio/haptic), Emergency stop\n\n### Principles: Intuitive, responsive, safe, informative, accessible", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Design robot controller UI. Wireframe first, then implement.\n\n**Misconception**: Aesthetics over functionality.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Sketch greenhouse UI: controls, displays, emergency, feedback."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y11", "topic": "Programming mechatronics", "section": "Programming and building", "content_text": "Create and use unit tests to determine the effectiveness and repeatability of each component's control algorithm", "explanation": "## Unit Testing in Mechatronics\n\n### What to Test: Sensor functions, motor control, conversions, control algorithms\n\n### Repeatability: Same test multiple times, consistent results expected. Identifies intermittent issues.\n\n### Benefits: Isolate problems, verify after changes, build confidence before integration, document behaviour, maintenance baselines", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this\n\n**Activity**: Unit tests before integration. No integration until all pass.\n\n**Repeatability**: Run sensor test 10 times, graph, discuss consistency.\n\n**Misconception**: Skip to integrated testing. Can't identify which component fails.\n\n**Quick Assessment**: Motor function - 5 unit tests (normal, boundary, error). Run 3x for repeatability."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Designing software", "content_text": "Describe the benefits of developing secure software", "explanation": "## Benefits of Developing Secure Software\n\nSecure software development is the practice of integrating security considerations into every phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), rather than treating security as an afterthought.\n\n### Key Benefits\n\n- **Reduced data breach costs**: The average cost of a data breach globally exceeds $4 million (IBM, 2023). Proactive security is far cheaper than reactive incident response.\n- **Regulatory compliance**: Organisations must comply with laws such as the Australian Privacy Act 1988, the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme, and GDPR for EU users. Non-compliance results in fines and legal action.\n- **User trust and reputation**: Users choose software they trust with their data. A breach can permanently damage brand reputation.\n- **Reduced technical debt**: Fixing security flaws in production costs 30x more than fixing them during design. Building security in from the start saves time and money.\n- **Business continuity**: Secure software is more resilient to attacks, reducing downtime and maintaining operations.\n- **Competitive advantage**: Security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2) can differentiate a product in the market.\n\n### Key Terminology\n\n- **SDLC**: Software Development Life Cycle — the structured process for developing software\n- **Technical debt**: The accumulated cost of shortcuts or deferred work in code\n- **NDB scheme**: Australian requirement to notify individuals and OAIC of eligible data breaches\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain benefits to an enterprise (financial, legal, reputational)\n- Compare costs of proactive vs reactive security\n- Link benefits to specific Australian legislation", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Analogy**: Compare secure software to building a house with fire-resistant materials vs adding fire extinguishers after construction. Prevention is cheaper and more effective than reaction.\n\n**Classroom Activity**: Present students with two scenarios — Company A spends $50,000 on security during development; Company B skips security and later suffers a breach costing $2 million in fines, remediation, and lost customers. Students calculate the ROI of security investment.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a table template where students list benefits in categories: Financial, Legal, Reputational, Technical. This organises their thinking.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students often think security only means 'stopping hackers'. Emphasise that it also includes data integrity, availability, and compliance.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: Exit ticket — 'Name three benefits of secure software development and explain which you think is most important for a hospital patient management system.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Designing software", "content_text": "Interpret and apply fundamental software development steps to develop secure code", "explanation": "## Fundamental Software Development Steps for Secure Code\n\nSecure code development follows the standard SDLC phases, with security activities integrated at every stage.\n\n### The Secure SDLC Phases\n\n1. **Requirements** — Identify security requirements alongside functional ones. Define data classification, access controls, compliance needs.\n2. **Design** — Apply security design principles: least privilege, defence in depth, fail-safe defaults, separation of duties. Create threat models.\n3. **Implementation** — Write secure code following coding standards. Use static analysis tools. Avoid common vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10).\n4. **Testing** — Conduct security testing: static analysis (SAST), dynamic analysis (DAST), penetration testing, code review.\n5. **Deployment** — Harden the production environment. Configure firewalls, TLS certificates, secure headers. Remove debug code.\n6. **Maintenance** — Monitor for vulnerabilities, apply patches, respond to incidents, review security logs.\n\n### Security at Each Step\n\n| Phase | Security Activity |\n|-------|-------------------|\n| Requirements | Threat modelling, compliance requirements |\n| Design | Secure architecture patterns, access control design |\n| Implementation | Secure coding practices, input validation |\n| Testing | SAST, DAST, penetration testing |\n| Deployment | Environment hardening, secure configuration |\n| Maintenance | Patch management, incident response |\n\n### Key Terminology\n\n- **Threat modelling**: Identifying potential threats and designing mitigations (e.g. STRIDE model)\n- **OWASP Top 10**: The 10 most critical web application security risks\n- **SAST/DAST**: Static/Dynamic Application Security Testing\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe security activities at each SDLC phase\n- Explain why security should be integrated throughout, not added at the end\n- Apply the steps to a given scenario", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Give students a simplified software project (e.g. a school library system). For each SDLC phase, they must identify at least one security consideration. Use a table with phases as rows and security activities as columns.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide the SDLC phases pre-filled and ask students to match security activities from a word bank to each phase.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think 'testing' is where all security happens. Emphasise that security is a thread running through ALL phases, not a single gate.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: Give students a scenario where a developer only adds security during testing. Ask them to identify what security issues might have been prevented if security was integrated earlier."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Designing software", "content_text": "Describe how the capabilities and experience of end users influence the secure design features of software", "explanation": "## How End User Capabilities Influence Secure Design\n\nThe security features of software must match the technical literacy, accessibility needs, and context of use of the target audience.\n\n### User Capability Spectrum\n\n- **Novice users** (elderly, non-technical): Need simple security interfaces, default-secure settings, clear plain-language guidance, and minimal decisions.\n- **Intermediate users** (general public): Need balance between security and convenience. Standard MFA, password managers, clear notifications.\n- **Expert users** (developers, IT professionals): May need granular security controls, API key management, audit logs, and advanced configuration.\n\n### Design Principles Based on User Capability\n\n- **Secure defaults**: Choose the most secure option as default, so users don't need to configure security. Example: enabling HTTPS by default.\n- **Progressive disclosure**: Show advanced security options only when users actively seek them, keeping the basic interface simple.\n- **Clear feedback**: Use plain language for security messages. Replace 'TLS certificate validation failed (ERR_CERT_COMMON_NAME_INVALID)' with 'This site may not be safe. The security certificate doesn't match.'\n- **Error recovery**: Provide clear steps to resolve security issues (e.g. 'Forgot password' flow with multiple verification options).\n- **Accessibility**: Security features must be usable with screen readers, keyboard navigation, and alternative input methods (WCAG 2.1).\n\n### Real-World Examples\n\n- Banking apps for elderly users use large text, simple MFA (SMS), and explicit confirmation dialogs.\n- Developer tools like AWS IAM provide complex permission policies with JSON configuration.\n- Children's apps use parental controls with PIN-protected settings.\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Given a target audience, recommend appropriate security features\n- Explain why one-size-fits-all security is problematic\n- Discuss the trade-off between security strength and usability", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Divide students into groups. Each group designs security features for a different user group: elderly banking app, children's game, developer API platform. They present and compare designs, noting how the same security goal (authentication) looks different for each audience.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a comparison table with user groups as columns and security features as rows (authentication, error messages, default settings). Students fill in appropriate approaches for each group.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think 'more secure = better' regardless of user. Explain that overly complex security drives users to bypass it (e.g. writing passwords on sticky notes).\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: Show students a screenshot of a complex security settings page. Ask: 'Is this appropriate for an elderly user? How would you redesign it?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Explore fundamental software design security concepts when developing programming code", "explanation": "## Fundamental Software Design Security Concepts\n\nThese are established principles that guide developers in creating inherently secure software.\n\n### Core Security Design Principles\n\n- **Least privilege**: Every user, process, and module should have only the minimum access needed to perform its function. Example: a reporting module should have read-only database access, not write access.\n- **Defence in depth**: Layer multiple security controls so if one fails, others still protect the system. Example: input validation + parameterised queries + WAF.\n- **Fail-safe defaults**: Systems should deny access by default and explicitly grant it. Example: a new user role has no permissions until they are specifically assigned.\n- **Separation of duties**: Critical operations require multiple actors. Example: one developer writes code, another reviews it before merging.\n- **Economy of mechanism**: Keep security mechanisms simple. Complex systems have more potential failure points.\n- **Complete mediation**: Every access request must be checked against access controls. Don't cache authorisation decisions.\n- **Open design**: Security should not depend on secrecy of the design (Kerckhoffs' principle). The system should be secure even if attackers know how it works.\n\n### Code Examples\n\n```python\n# Least Privilege - database connection with read-only access\ndb_connection = connect(user='report_reader', password=pwd, \n database='analytics', readonly=True)\n\n# Fail-safe default - new user has no permissions\nclass User:\n def __init__(self, name):\n self.name = name\n self.permissions = [] # No access by default\n```\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Define and give examples of security design principles\n- Identify which principle is violated in a code scenario\n- Apply principles to a given software design problem", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Analogy**: Compare defence in depth to a castle — moat, walls, guards, locked doors. If one layer fails, the next protects.\n\n**Activity**: Give students insecure code snippets and ask them to identify which security principle is violated and how to fix it.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students confuse 'security through obscurity' with real security. Emphasise that hiding source code is NOT a security measure — proper design principles are.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: Match-up exercise — match each principle name to its definition and a code example."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Apply security features incorporated into software including data protection, security, privacy and regulatory compliance", "explanation": "## Security Features: Data Protection, Privacy, and Compliance\n\nModern software must incorporate multiple layers of security features to protect data and meet legal obligations.\n\n### Data Protection Features\n\n- **Encryption at rest**: Encrypt stored data using AES-256.
