Databases, file systems, and backups should all be encrypted.\n- **Encryption in transit**: Use TLS 1.3/HTTPS for all network communication. Never transmit sensitive data over plain HTTP.\n- **Access controls**: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) or Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) to restrict who can access what data.\n- **Data masking**: Display partial data (e.g. credit card: **** **** **** 1234) in logs and UIs.\n\n### Privacy Features\n\n- **Consent management**: Collect explicit user consent before processing personal data. Provide opt-out mechanisms.\n- **Data minimisation**: Only collect data that is strictly necessary for the stated purpose.\n- **Right to deletion**: Implement functionality for users to request deletion of their personal data.\n- **Anonymisation/Pseudonymisation**: Remove or replace identifiers when data is used for analytics.\n\n### Regulatory Compliance\n\n- **Australian Privacy Act 1988**: 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) governing personal information handling.\n- **NDB scheme**: Mandatory notification of eligible data breaches to OAIC and affected individuals.\n- **GDPR** (if serving EU users): Strict consent requirements, data portability, right to be forgotten.\n- **PCI DSS** (if handling credit cards): Standards for secure payment processing.\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- List security features and explain how they protect data\n- Connect features to specific legislation\n- Evaluate whether a given system meets compliance requirements", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Present a fictional app (e.g. a health tracking app). Students audit it against a compliance checklist: Does it encrypt data? Does it have consent management? Does it comply with the Privacy Act?\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a checklist template with categories (Data Protection, Privacy, Compliance) and have students tick off features and note gaps.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think encryption alone means the software is 'secure'. Emphasise that security is multi-layered — encryption, access controls, privacy features, and compliance all work together.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: Give a scenario where a company collects user data without consent. Ask students to identify which APP (Australian Privacy Principle) is violated."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Use and explain the contribution of cryptography and sandboxing to the 'security by design' approach", "explanation": "## Cryptography and Sandboxing in Security by Design\n\nSecurity by design means building security into the architecture from the start, not bolting it on later. Cryptography and sandboxing are two foundational pillars.\n\n### Cryptography\n\nCryptography transforms readable data (plaintext) into unreadable form (ciphertext) and back, protecting confidentiality and integrity.\n\n- **Symmetric encryption** (AES-256): Same key encrypts and decrypts. Fast, used for data at rest and bulk data.\n- **Asymmetric encryption** (RSA, ECC): Public key encrypts, private key decrypts. Used for key exchange, digital signatures.\n- **Hashing** (SHA-256, bcrypt): One-way function producing a fixed-size digest. Used for password storage and data integrity verification.\n- **Digital signatures**: Verify the sender's identity and that data hasn't been tampered with.\n\n```python\n# Hashing a password with bcrypt (one-way, salted)\nimport bcrypt\nhashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt())\n\n# Symmetric encryption with Fernet (AES-128-CBC)\nfrom cryptography.fernet import Fernet\nkey = Fernet.generate_key()\ncipher = Fernet(key)\nencrypted = cipher.encrypt(b'sensitive data')\n```\n\n### Sandboxing\n\nSandboxing isolates running programs in a restricted environment, limiting access to system resources.\n\n- **Browser sandboxes**: Each tab runs in an isolated process — a compromised tab cannot access other tabs or system files.\n- **Container sandboxes** (Docker): Applications run in isolated containers with limited filesystem, network, and process access.\n- **Virtual machines**: Complete OS isolation for running untrusted code.\n- **Language sandboxes**: Java's SecurityManager, Python's restricted execution environments.\n\n### Contribution to Security by Design\n\nBoth are **proactive** — they are designed into the system architecture, not added as patches. Cryptography protects data even if perimeters are breached. Sandboxing limits damage even if code is compromised.\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Compare symmetric vs asymmetric encryption use cases\n- Explain how sandboxing limits the impact of a security breach\n- Give examples of each in real-world systems", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Analogy**: Cryptography is like putting a letter in a locked box — only the person with the key can read it. Sandboxing is like a quarantine room — even if something dangerous is inside, it can't get out.\n\n**Activity**: Students encrypt and decrypt messages using a simple Python script with the cryptography library. Then discuss: what happens if the key is lost?\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think hashing is encryption. Emphasise that hashing is one-way (you can't decrypt a hash) while encryption is two-way.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Is bcrypt encryption or hashing? Why is this distinction important for password storage?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Use and explain the 'privacy by design' approach in the development of software solutions", "explanation": "## Privacy by Design\n\nPrivacy by Design (PbD) is a framework developed by Ann Cavoukian requiring privacy to be embedded into system design and business practices from the outset.\n\n### The 7 Foundational Principles\n\n1. **Proactive not reactive**: Anticipate and prevent privacy invasions before they occur.\n2. **Privacy as the default**: Personal data is automatically protected — users don't need to take action.\n3. **Privacy embedded into design**: Privacy is a core component of the system architecture, not an add-on.\n4. **Full functionality (positive-sum)**: Privacy and functionality are not trade-offs — achieve both.\n5. **End-to-end security**: Full lifecycle protection from data collection to deletion.\n6. **Visibility and transparency**: Keep practices open, documented, and verifiable.\n7. **Respect for user privacy**: Keep the user's interests central — user-centred design.\n\n### Implementation in Software\n\n- **Data minimisation**: Only collect what is necessary. If you don't need a user's date of birth, don't ask for it.\n- **Purpose limitation**: Use data only for the stated purpose and delete it when no longer needed.\n- **Consent mechanisms**: Clear opt-in (not opt-out) for data collection with granular choices.\n- **Privacy dashboards**: Give users visibility into what data is held and controls to manage it.\n- **Anonymisation**: Strip identifiers from analytical data.\n\n```python\n# Privacy by Design - data minimisation example\nclass UserRegistration:\n # Only collect what's needed\n required_fields = ['email', 'password']\n # DON'T collect: date_of_birth, phone, address (unless needed)\n \n def register(self, data):\n # Strip any extra fields not in required_fields\n clean_data = {k: v for k, v in data.items() \n if k in self.required_fields}\n return self.save(clean_data)\n```\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- List and explain the 7 principles\n- Apply PbD to a scenario (e.g. designing a health app)\n- Contrast PbD with 'privacy as an afterthought'", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Give students a registration form that collects 15 fields of personal data. They must identify which fields are truly necessary and redesign the form applying data minimisation.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide the 7 principles as a checklist. Students audit a given app against each principle and rate compliance.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think privacy by design just means 'adding a privacy policy page'. Emphasise it's about architectural decisions, not legal documents.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A social media app collects location data even when the user is not posting. Which PbD principle does this violate and how would you fix it?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Test and evaluate the security and resilience of software by determining vulnerabilities, hardening systems, handling breaches, maintaining business continuity and conducting disaster recovery", "explanation": "## Testing Security and Resilience\n\nThis dot point covers the full spectrum of security operations: finding weaknesses, strengthening defences, responding to incidents, and recovering from disasters.\n\n### Determining Vulnerabilities\n\n- **Penetration testing**: Simulated attacks by ethical hackers to find exploitable flaws.\n- **Vulnerability scanning**: Automated tools (e.g. Nessus, OWASP ZAP) that scan for known vulnerabilities.\n- **Static Application Security Testing (SAST)**: Analyses source code for security flaws without running it.\n- **Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)**: Tests the running application for vulnerabilities.\n- **Code review**: Manual peer examination of code for security issues.\n\n### Hardening Systems\n\n- Disable unnecessary services and ports\n- Apply all security patches and updates\n- Configure firewalls and intrusion detection systems\n- Remove default accounts and passwords\n- Implement least privilege access\n- Enable logging and monitoring\n\n### Handling Breaches\n\n1. **Contain**: Isolate affected systems to prevent spread\n2. **Assess**: Determine scope — what data was compromised, how many users affected\n3. **Notify**: Inform affected individuals and OAIC (under NDB scheme) within 30 days\n4. **Remediate**: Patch the exploited vulnerability\n5. **Review**: Post-incident analysis to improve defences\n\n### Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery\n\n- **BCP (Business Continuity Plan)**: Ensures the organisation can continue operating during disruption\n- **DRP (Disaster Recovery Plan)**: Specifically focuses on restoring IT systems after a catastrophe\n- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)**: Maximum acceptable downtime\n- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)**: Maximum acceptable data loss (determines backup frequency)\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe the difference between BCP and DRP\n- Outline steps to handle a data breach\n- Compare vulnerability testing methods", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Run a tabletop exercise — present a breach scenario (e.g. 'Customer database leaked via SQL injection'). Students role-play as the incident response team and walk through containment, assessment, notification, and recovery steps.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide an incident response template with blank fields for each step. Students fill in actions, responsible parties, and timelines.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think a firewall alone makes a system 'hardened'. Emphasise that hardening involves multiple actions across the entire system.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Define RTO and RPO. If a hospital has an RTO of 1 hour and RPO of 15 minutes, what does this mean for their backup strategy?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Apply and evaluate strategies used by software developers to manage the security of programming code", "explanation": "## Strategies for Managing Code Security\n\nDevelopers use multiple strategies to ensure their code remains secure throughout its lifecycle.\n\n### Key Strategies\n\n- **Static code analysis**: Tools like SonarQube, ESLint (security rules), Bandit (Python) automatically scan code for vulnerabilities during development. Integrated into CI/CD pipelines.\n- **Code reviews**: Peer examination before merging. Reviewers check for security issues like hardcoded credentials, SQL injection, and improper error handling.\n- **Dependency management**: Regularly audit third-party libraries for known vulnerabilities using tools like npm audit, Dependabot, or Snyk.\n- **Secure coding standards**: Following guidelines like OWASP Secure Coding Practices, CERT Secure Coding Standards.\n- **Secret management**: Never hardcode API keys, passwords, or tokens in source code. Use environment variables or secret managers (e.g. AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault).\n- **Security training**: Regular training for developers on emerging threats and secure coding practices.\n\n### CI/CD Security Integration\n\n```yaml\n# Example: GitHub Actions security pipeline\nsteps:\n - name: Run SAST\n run: bandit -r src/\n - name: Check dependencies\n run: pip audit\n - name: Run secrets scanner\n run: trufflehog filesystem ./\n```\n\n### Evaluation Criteria\n\nWhen evaluating strategies, consider:\n- **Effectiveness**: Does it catch the vulnerabilities it targets?\n- **Coverage**: Does it cover the relevant attack surface?\n- **Automation**: Can it run without human intervention?\n- **False positives**: Does it generate excessive noise?\n- **Cost**: Is it justified for the project's risk profile?\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Compare automated vs manual security strategies\n- Evaluate the effectiveness of a specific strategy for a given context\n- Describe how strategies integrate into the development workflow", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students set up a simple GitHub repository with a pre-commit hook that runs a Python linter with security rules (bandit). They intentionally write insecure code and see the tool catch it.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a comparison table template: Strategy | What it does | Automated? | Catches what? | Limitations\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think one tool/strategy is enough. Emphasise defence in depth — multiple strategies catch different types of issues.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A developer commits an API key to a public GitHub repository. Which strategy would have prevented this? Name the strategy and a specific tool.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement code using defensive data input handling practices, including input validation, sanitisation and error handling", "explanation": "## Defensive Data Input Handling\n\nDefensive input handling is the practice of never trusting user input. All data entering a system must be validated, sanitised, and error-handled.\n\n### The Three Pillars\n\n**1. Input Validation** — Checking that input meets expected criteria:\n- Type checking (is it a number?)\n- Range checking (is age between 0-150?)\n- Length checking (is username 3-50 characters?)\n- Format checking (does email match regex?)\n- Whitelist validation (is selection from allowed values?)\n\n**2. Input Sanitisation** — Cleaning input to remove harmful content:\n- Stripping HTML tags to prevent XSS\n- Escaping special characters in SQL\n- Removing path traversal characters (../, ~)\n- Normalising unicode characters\n\n**3. Error Handling** — Gracefully managing invalid input:\n- Try-except blocks to catch exceptions\n- Meaningful error messages for users\n- Minimal error details in responses (don't leak internals)\n- Logging errors for developers\n\n### Code Examples\n\n```python\nimport re\nimport html\n\ndef process_comment(user_input):\n # VALIDATION\n if not isinstance(user_input, str):\n raise ValueError('Input must be a string')\n if len(user_input) > 500:\n raise ValueError('Comment must be under 500 characters')\n \n # SANITISATION\n clean = html.escape(user_input) # Escape HTML entities\n clean = clean.strip() # Remove leading/trailing whitespace\n \n # ERROR HANDLING\n try:\n save_to_database(clean)\n except DatabaseError as e:\n log_error(e) # Log full error for developers\n raise UserError('Could not save comment. Please try again.') # Safe message for user\n```\n\n### Common Vulnerabilities Prevented\n\n- **SQL Injection**: Use parameterised queries, never string concatenation\n- **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)**: Escape HTML output, use textContent not innerHTML\n- **Path Traversal**: Validate file paths, use whitelists\n- **Buffer Overflow**: Enforce input length limits\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Identify vulnerabilities in given code and apply fixes\n- Distinguish between validation, sanitisation, and error handling\n- Write secure input handling code", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Give students deliberately vulnerable code (a login form with SQL injection, a comment box with XSS). They must identify the vulnerability, explain the attack, and write the secure version.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a three-column template: Original Code | Vulnerability | Secure Version. Students complete each row.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think client-side JavaScript validation is sufficient. Emphasise that client-side validation can be bypassed — server-side validation is mandatory.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Why is this code vulnerable? `query = \"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = \" + user_id`. Write the secure version using parameterised queries.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement a safe application programming interface (API) to minimise software vulnerabilities", "explanation": "## Safe API Design\n\nAPIs are a major attack surface. A safe API minimises vulnerabilities through authentication, authorisation, validation, and monitoring.\n\n### Key API Security Features\n\n- **Authentication**: Verify caller identity using API keys, OAuth 2.0 tokens, or JWTs.\n- **Authorisation**: Ensure authenticated users can only access resources they're permitted to (RBAC).\n- **Input validation**: Validate all parameters — type, length, format, range. Reject invalid requests (HTTP 400).\n- **Rate limiting**: Restrict requests per time window (e.g. 100/min) to prevent abuse and DDoS.\n- **HTTPS only**: Enforce TLS encryption. Reject plain HTTP connections.\n- **Minimal error responses**: Return generic error messages. Never expose stack traces, SQL queries, or internal paths.\n- **CORS configuration**: Restrict which origins can call the API.\n- **Versioning**: Use API versioning (e.g. /api/v1/) to maintain backward compatibility.\n\n### Example: Secure vs Insecure API\n\n```python\n# INSECURE API endpoint\n@app.route('/api/user')\ndef get_user():\n user_id = request.args.get('id')\n query = f\"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}\" # SQL injection!\n result = db.execute(query)\n return jsonify(result) # Returns ALL fields including password hash\n\n# SECURE API endpoint\n@app.route('/api/v1/user')\n@require_auth # Authentication\n@rate_limit(100, per=60) # Rate limiting\ndef get_user():\n user_id = request.args.get('id')\n if not user_id or not user_id.isdigit(): # Validation\n return jsonify({'error': 'Invalid user ID'}), 400\n result = db.execute('SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE id = ?', \n (int(user_id),)) # Parameterised, limited fields\n if not result:\n return jsonify({'error': 'Not found'}), 404\n return jsonify(result)\n```\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Compare secure vs insecure API code\n- List API security features and explain each\n- Design an API endpoint with security features for a given scenario", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students design a simple REST API for a library system (search books, borrow books, return books). For each endpoint, they must specify: HTTP method, URL, authentication, input validation rules, and error responses.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide an API specification template with columns: Endpoint | Method | Auth Required | Input Validation | Error Responses.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think API keys in URLs are safe. Explain that URLs are logged in server logs, browser history, and referrer headers — use headers instead.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'What is wrong with this API response? `{\"error\": \"SQL Error: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = abc — invalid column type\"}`. How should the error be returned instead?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement code considering efficient execution for the user", "explanation": "## Efficient Code Execution\n\nEfficient code delivers results quickly while using minimal system resources. This directly impacts user experience and can have security implications.\n\n### Why Efficiency Matters\n\n- **User experience**: Users abandon pages that take more than 3 seconds to load\n- **Security**: Inefficient code can be exploited for denial-of-service attacks\n- **Cost**: Inefficient cloud-hosted code costs more in compute resources\n- **Scalability**: Efficient code handles more concurrent users\n\n### Efficiency Techniques\n\n- **Algorithm optimisation**: Choose appropriate time complexity. O(n log n) sort vs O(n^2) matters at scale.\n- **Caching**: Store frequently accessed data in memory (Redis, Memcached) rather than recomputing or re-querying.\n- **Lazy loading**: Load resources only when needed (images, modules, data).\n- **Database optimisation**: Use indexes, avoid N+1 queries, use pagination.\n- **Asynchronous operations**: Don't block the main thread. Use async/await for I/O operations.\n\n```python\n# INEFFICIENT - O(n^2) nested loop\ndef find_duplicates_slow(items):\n duplicates = []\n for i in range(len(items)):\n for j in range(i+1, len(items)):\n if items[i] == items[j]:\n duplicates.append(items[i])\n return duplicates\n\n# EFFICIENT - O(n) using a set\ndef find_duplicates_fast(items):\n seen = set()\n duplicates = set()\n for item in items:\n if item in seen:\n duplicates.add(item)\n seen.add(item)\n return list(duplicates)\n```\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Compare two code solutions for efficiency\n- Identify inefficiencies in given code\n- Explain how inefficient code creates security vulnerabilities", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students time two versions of the same algorithm (e.g. linear search vs binary search) on a large dataset using Python's time module. They graph the results to visualise O(n) vs O(log n).\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide both efficient and inefficient code side by side. Students annotate the differences and explain why one is faster.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think modern computers are fast enough that efficiency doesn't matter. Show them how O(n^2) on 1 million records takes minutes while O(n) takes milliseconds.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'This function searches a sorted list using a for loop from start to end. What is its time complexity? How would you improve it?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement secure code to minimise vulnerabilities in user action controls", "explanation": "## Secure User Action Controls\n\nUser action controls govern what users can do within an application. Vulnerabilities here allow attackers to perform actions as other users or bypass restrictions.\n\n### Key Vulnerabilities\n\n- **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)**: Tricks a user's browser into making unintended requests using their authenticated session.\n- **Broken Access Control**: Users access resources or perform actions beyond their authorised permissions (e.g. accessing /admin without admin role).\n- **Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)**: Manipulating IDs in URLs to access other users' data (e.g. changing /user/123 to /user/124).\n- **Privilege escalation**: Regular users gain admin privileges through parameter manipulation.\n\n### Secure Implementation\n\n```python\n# CSRF Protection - include token in forms\nfrom flask_wtf.csrf import CSRFProtect\ncsrf = CSRFProtect(app)\n\n# Access Control - check permissions\n@app.route('/admin/users')\n@login_required\ndef admin_users():\n if current_user.role != 'admin':\n abort(403) # Forbidden\n return render_admin_page()\n\n# IDOR Prevention - verify ownership\n@app.route('/api/order/')\n@login_required\ndef get_order(order_id):\n order = Order.query.get(order_id)\n if order.user_id != current_user.id:\n abort(403) # User doesn't own this order\n return jsonify(order.to_dict())\n```\n\n### Mitigation Strategies\n\n- Anti-CSRF tokens in all state-changing forms\n- Server-side authorisation checks on every request\n- Indirect object references (map internal IDs to random tokens)\n- Re-authentication for sensitive actions (e.g. changing password)\n- SameSite cookie attribute to prevent cross-origin cookie sending\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain CSRF and how to prevent it\n- Identify access control vulnerabilities in code\n- Write code that implements proper authorisation checks", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Demonstrate CSRF with a simple example — show how a malicious page with a hidden form could transfer money from a logged-in banking session. Then show the fix (CSRF tokens).\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a vulnerable web app snippet and a checklist of security controls (CSRF token, access check, ownership verification). Students apply each fix.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think being logged in means all actions are legitimate. Explain that CSRF exploits the trust between the server and the user's browser.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A user changes the URL from /profile/5 to /profile/6 and sees another user's data. What is this vulnerability called and how do you fix it?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Developing secure code", "content_text": "Design, develop and implement secure code to protect user file and hardware vulnerabilities from file attacks and side channel attacks", "explanation": "## Protecting Against File Attacks and Side-Channel Attacks\n\nFile attacks target the file upload/download mechanisms of applications. Side-channel attacks extract information from the physical implementation of a system.\n\n### File Attack Types\n\n- **Malicious file upload**: Uploading executable files (PHP, JS) disguised as images that run on the server.\n- **Path traversal**: Using sequences like `../../etc/passwd` in filenames to access restricted files.\n- **Zip bombs**: Compressed files that expand to enormous sizes, crashing the server.\n- **XML External Entity (XXE)**: Malicious XML files that read server files or make network requests.\n\n### File Upload Security\n\n```python\nimport os\nimport magic # python-magic library\n\nALLOWED_TYPES = {'image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/gif'}\nMAX_SIZE = 5 * 1024 * 1024 # 5MB\n\ndef secure_upload(file):\n # Check file size\n if file.content_length > MAX_SIZE:\n raise ValueError('File too large')\n \n # Validate MIME type using magic bytes (not extension)\n mime = magic.from_buffer(file.read(2048), mime=True)\n file.seek(0)\n if mime not in ALLOWED_TYPES:\n raise ValueError('Invalid file type')\n \n # Sanitise filename - remove path separators\n safe_name = secure_filename(file.filename)\n # Generate unique name to prevent overwrites\n unique_name = f\"{uuid4().hex}_{safe_name}\"\n # Store outside web root\n path = os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, unique_name)\n file.save(path)\n```\n\n### Side-Channel Attacks\n\n- **Timing attacks**: Measuring response time to deduce information (e.g. password character-by-character guessing)\n- **Power analysis**: Monitoring power consumption to extract cryptographic keys\n- **Cache attacks**: Exploiting CPU cache behaviour to read other processes' data (e.g. Spectre, Meltdown)\n\n### Mitigation\n\n- Use constant-time comparison functions for secrets\n- Implement rate limiting to slow brute-force timing attacks\n- Keep systems patched against known side-channel vulnerabilities\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe file upload vulnerabilities and mitigations\n- Explain what a side-channel attack is with examples\n- Write secure file upload code", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students attempt to upload a file with a .php extension renamed to .jpg. They then inspect it with a hex editor to see that the magic bytes don't match JPEG. This demonstrates why extension-based validation is insufficient.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide an insecure file upload function and a list of security measures. Students add each measure step by step.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think checking the file extension is enough. Show that renaming a .php file to .jpg doesn't change its content — magic byte validation is essential.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'What is a timing attack? Give an example of how an attacker could exploit a password comparison that exits early on the first wrong character.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Impact of safe and secure software development", "content_text": "Apply and describe the benefits of collaboration to develop safe and secure software", "explanation": "## Benefits of Collaboration in Secure Development\n\nCollaboration brings diverse perspectives and expertise that significantly improve software security.\n\n### How Collaboration Improves Security\n\n- **Code reviews**: Peers catch security issues the original developer missed. Studies show code reviews find 60% of defects before testing.\n- **Pair programming**: Two developers working together catch errors in real-time and share security knowledge.\n- **Cross-functional teams**: Including security specialists, QA, and operations in development teams (DevSecOps) ensures security is considered from all angles.\n- **Threat modelling workshops**: Collaborative sessions where team members identify potential threats from different perspectives (developer, attacker, user).\n- **Knowledge sharing**: Team members with different backgrounds share security knowledge — a database specialist might spot SQL issues a front-end developer wouldn't.\n\n### Collaboration Tools for Security\n\n- **Version control (Git)**: Pull requests enable code review before merging\n- **CI/CD pipelines**: Automated security checks that all team members benefit from\n- **Issue trackers (Jira)**: Track and prioritise security issues\n- **Communication (Slack, Teams)**: Security alerts and discussions\n- **Documentation (Wiki/Confluence)**: Shared security guidelines and incident playbooks\n\n### Benefits to the Development Process\n\n- Reduces single points of failure (bus factor)\n- Distributes security knowledge across the team\n- Creates accountability through transparency\n- Enables faster incident response through shared context\n- Improves code quality through diverse review\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain how collaboration improves security outcomes\n- Describe specific collaboration practices and their security benefits\n- Evaluate the effectiveness of collaboration tools in a security context", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students work in pairs to review each other's code for security vulnerabilities. One student writes code with intentional flaws, the other must find and fix them through a simulated pull request.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a code review checklist with security items: input validation, error handling, authentication checks, hardcoded secrets, SQL injection.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think working alone is faster. Show that solo developers miss significantly more security issues than teams.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Name two collaboration practices that improve software security and explain why they work better than one developer working alone.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Impact of safe and secure software development", "content_text": "Investigate and explain the benefits to an enterprise of the implementation of safe and secure development practices", "explanation": "## Enterprise Benefits of Secure Development\n\nSecure development practices deliver tangible business value beyond just preventing attacks.\n\n### Financial Benefits\n\n- **Reduced breach costs**: Prevention costs a fraction of remediation. Average breach costs exceed $4 million.\n- **Lower insurance premiums**: Demonstrating security practices can reduce cyber insurance costs.\n- **Avoided regulatory fines**: Non-compliance with the Privacy Act can result in penalties up to $50 million for serious breaches.\n- **Reduced downtime costs**: Secure systems experience fewer outages from attacks.\n\n### Legal and Compliance Benefits\n\n- **Regulatory compliance**: Meeting requirements of Privacy Act 1988, NDB scheme, industry standards (PCI DSS, ISO 27001).\n- **Reduced legal liability**: Demonstrating reasonable security measures provides legal defence if a breach occurs.\n- **Contract eligibility**: Many government and enterprise contracts require security certifications.\n\n### Reputational Benefits\n\n- **Customer trust**: Users choose and remain loyal to companies that protect their data.\n- **Brand differentiation**: Security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2) serve as competitive differentiators.\n- **Stakeholder confidence**: Investors and partners are more confident in security-mature organisations.\n\n### Operational Benefits\n\n- **Reduced technical debt**: Building security in from the start avoids costly refactoring.\n- **Faster development**: Established security practices and reusable components speed up future projects.\n- **Better talent attraction**: Skilled developers prefer working in security-conscious organisations.\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- List and categorise benefits (financial, legal, reputational, operational)\n- Argue the business case for security investment\n- Calculate ROI of security practices vs breach costs", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students role-play as executives in a board meeting. One group argues for investing $200K in security practices; another group argues it's unnecessary. They present data (breach statistics, compliance requirements) to support their position.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a cost-benefit analysis template with categories: Investment Cost, Potential Breach Cost, Regulatory Fines, Reputation Impact, Customer Retention.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think security is only a cost centre. Show that it directly enables revenue through customer trust and contract eligibility.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A small business asks: Why should we invest in security when we've never been attacked? Give three arguments.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Secure software architecture", "section": "Impact of safe and secure software development", "content_text": "Evaluate the social, ethical and legal issues and ramifications that affect people and enterprises resulting from the development and implementation of safe and secure software", "explanation": "## Social, Ethical and Legal Issues in Secure Software\n\nSecure software development intersects with broader societal concerns about privacy, access, fairness, and regulation.\n\n### Social Issues\n\n- **Digital divide**: Highly secure systems may exclude less tech-literate users who cannot navigate complex authentication.\n- **Surveillance**: Security features (logging, monitoring, facial recognition) can enable surveillance of employees and citizens.\n- **Trust erosion**: High-profile breaches erode public trust in digital services.\n- **Accessibility**: Security measures (CAPTCHAs, complex passwords) can create barriers for users with disabilities.\n\n### Ethical Issues\n\n- **Privacy vs security trade-off**: More monitoring increases security but reduces privacy. Where is the ethical line?\n- **Responsible disclosure**: Should security researchers publicly disclose vulnerabilities? Balanced approach: notify the vendor first, allow time to patch, then disclose.\n- **Data collection ethics**: Is it ethical to collect more data than needed for 'security purposes'?\n- **AI bias in security**: Automated security systems (e.g. fraud detection) may discriminate against certain demographics.\n- **Encryption debate**: Law enforcement argues encryption hinders criminal investigations; privacy advocates argue backdoors weaken everyone's security.\n\n### Legal Issues\n\n- **Privacy Act 1988 (Cth)**: 13 APPs governing personal information handling in Australia.\n- **NDB scheme**: Mandatory breach notification requirements.\n- **Computer crimes**: Criminal Code Act 1995 — unauthorised access, modification, impairment of electronic communications.\n- **Intellectual property**: Copyright protects source code; patents may protect algorithms.\n- **International law**: GDPR applies to any software processing EU residents' data, regardless of where the company is based.\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Evaluate a scenario for social, ethical, and legal issues\n- Discuss the privacy vs security trade-off\n- Apply Australian legislation to a data breach scenario", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Debate format — 'Should the government have a backdoor to all encrypted communications?' Students research and argue both sides, considering social, ethical, and legal perspectives.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a SEEL framework: Social, Ethical, Economic, Legal. Students categorise issues under each heading for a given scenario.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think 'legal' and 'ethical' are the same thing. Something can be legal but unethical (e.g. collecting excessive data with buried consent), or ethical but legally unclear.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A fitness app shares user health data with advertisers. Identify one social, one ethical, and one legal issue with this practice.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Data transmission using the web", "content_text": "Explore the applications of web programming", "explanation": "## Applications of Web Programming\n\nWeb programming encompasses all software development that runs on or interacts with the World Wide Web.\n\n### Categories of Web Applications\n\n- **E-commerce platforms**: Online stores (Shopify, custom builds) with product catalogues, shopping carts, payment processing.\n- **Social media**: Platforms like Facebook, Twitter, Reddit — real-time content sharing, messaging, user-generated content.\n- **SaaS (Software as a Service)**: Cloud-based tools accessed via browser — Google Docs, Canva, Slack, Trello.\n- **Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)**: Web apps with offline capability and native-like experience (e.g. Twitter Lite, Starbucks).\n- **RESTful APIs and microservices**: Backend services that provide data to mobile apps, IoT devices, and other web applications.\n- **Content Management Systems**: WordPress, Drupal — platforms for creating and managing web content.\n- **Real-time applications**: Chat applications (WebSocket), collaborative editing (Google Docs), live streaming.\n- **Web-based games**: Browser games using Canvas API, WebGL, or frameworks like Phaser.\n- **Data dashboards**: Business intelligence tools visualising data in real-time (e.g. Grafana, custom dashboards).\n\n### Technologies Used\n\n- **Front-end**: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Vue.js, Angular\n- **Back-end**: Node.js, Python (Django/Flask), PHP, Ruby, Java (Spring)\n- **Databases**: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis\n- **Infrastructure**: AWS, Azure, GCP, Docker, Kubernetes\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- List and describe applications of web programming\n- Match technologies to application types\n- Discuss the growth and significance of web applications", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students brainstorm 10 web applications they use daily and categorise them (e-commerce, social media, SaaS, etc.). They then research the technology stack of one application.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a categorisation table with example applications pre-filled for each category. Students add more examples.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think web programming only means 'making websites'. Emphasise that APIs, backend services, and real-time systems are all web programming.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Name three different types of web applications and give a real-world example of each.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Data transmission using the web", "content_text": "Investigate and practise how data is transferred on the internet", "explanation": "## Data Transfer on the Internet\n\nAll internet communication follows the TCP/IP model, a layered architecture where each layer has specific responsibilities.\n\n### TCP/IP Model (4 Layers)\n\n| Layer | Protocol Examples | Function |\n|-------|-------------------|----------|\n| Application | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS | Formats data for specific services |\n| Transport | TCP, UDP | Reliable delivery (TCP) or fast delivery (UDP) |\n| Internet | IP, ICMP | Addressing and routing packets |\n| Network Access | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | Physical data transmission |\n\n### How a Web Request Works\n\n1. User types `https://example.com` in browser\n2. **DNS resolution**: Browser resolves domain to IP address (93.184.216.34)\n3. **TCP handshake**: Three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) establishes connection\n4. **TLS handshake**: Negotiates encryption for HTTPS\n5. **HTTP request**: Browser sends GET request with headers\n6. **Server processing**: Server handles request, queries database if needed\n7. **HTTP response**: Server returns HTML, status code (200 OK), headers\n8. **Rendering**: Browser parses HTML, requests CSS/JS/images, renders page\n\n### TCP vs UDP\n\n- **TCP**: Reliable, ordered delivery. Used for web pages, email, file transfer. Guarantees every packet arrives.\n- **UDP**: Fast, no guarantees. Used for video streaming, gaming, DNS lookups. Accepts some packet loss for speed.\n\n### Packet Switching\n\nData is broken into packets, each routed independently through the network. Packets may take different paths and arrive out of order — TCP reassembles them correctly.\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe the TCP/IP layers and their functions\n- Trace a web request through all stages\n- Compare TCP and UDP with use cases", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students use browser DevTools Network tab to observe a real HTTP request — they see the URL, method, headers, status code, timing, and response body. Discuss each component.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a diagram of the TCP/IP model with blank labels. Students fill in layer names, protocols, and functions.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think data travels as a single block. Use the postal analogy — a book is split into pages (packets), each in a separate envelope, sent via different routes, and reassembled at the destination.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Would you use TCP or UDP for a live video call? Explain your choice.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Data transmission using the web", "content_text": "Investigate and describe the function of web protocols and their ports", "explanation": "## Web Protocols and Their Ports\n\nProtocols are standardised rules for data communication. Each protocol typically operates on a specific port number.\n\n### Key Protocols and Ports\n\n| Protocol | Port | Function |\n|----------|------|----------|\n| HTTP | 80 | Unencrypted web page transfer |\n| HTTPS | 443 | Encrypted web page transfer (HTTP over TLS) |\n| FTP | 21 (control), 20 (data) | File transfer |\n| SFTP/SCP | 22 | Secure file transfer over SSH |\n| SMTP | 25 (or 587 with TLS) | Sending email |\n| POP3 | 110 (or 995 with TLS) | Retrieving email (downloads) |\n| IMAP | 143 (or 993 with TLS) | Retrieving email (syncs) |\n| DNS | 53 | Domain name resolution |\n| SSH | 22 | Secure remote shell access |\n| WebSocket | 80/443 | Full-duplex real-time communication |\n\n### How Ports Work\n\nA port is a 16-bit number (0-65535) that identifies a specific service on a server. When a browser connects to `https://example.com`, it connects to IP address 93.184.216.34 on port 443.\n\n- **Well-known ports (0-1023)**: Reserved for standard protocols\n- **Registered ports (1024-49151)**: Used by specific applications\n- **Dynamic ports (49152-65535)**: Temporarily assigned for client connections\n\n### Protocol Functions\n\n- **HTTP/HTTPS**: Request-response protocol for web resources. HTTPS adds TLS encryption.\n- **FTP**: Transfers files between client and server. SFTP is the secure version.\n- **SMTP**: Sends email from client to mail server and between mail servers.\n- **DNS**: Translates domain names to IP addresses.\n- **WebSocket**: Enables persistent two-way communication (used in chat, live updates).\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Match protocols to their port numbers\n- Explain the function of specific protocols\n- Describe why HTTPS replaced HTTP as the standard", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students use `nmap` or browser DevTools to see which ports are being used when visiting various websites. They create a reference card of protocols and ports.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a matching exercise — protocol names on one side, port numbers and descriptions on the other.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think ports are physical connectors. Clarify that network ports are logical (software) identifiers, unlike USB/HDMI physical ports.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: Flash cards — show a port number, students name the protocol and its function."}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Data transmission using the web", "content_text": "Explain the processes for securing the web", "explanation": "## Processes for Securing the Web\n\nWeb security involves multiple layers of protection for data in transit, at rest, and during processing.\n\n### TLS/SSL (Transport Layer Security)\n\nTLS encrypts communication between browser and server:\n1. **Client Hello**: Browser sends supported cipher suites and TLS version\n2. **Server Hello**: Server selects cipher suite, sends its SSL certificate\n3. **Certificate verification**: Browser verifies certificate with CA (Certificate Authority)\n4. **Key exchange**: Asymmetric encryption exchanges a symmetric session key\n5. **Encrypted communication**: All subsequent data uses fast symmetric encryption\n\n### SSL Certificates\n\n- Issued by Certificate Authorities (CAs) like Let's Encrypt, DigiCert\n- Types: Domain Validation (DV), Organisation Validation (OV), Extended Validation (EV)\n- Verify the server's identity and enable HTTPS\n\n### Additional Web Security Measures\n\n- **HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)**: Forces browsers to only use HTTPS\n- **Content Security Policy (CSP)**: Restricts which resources a page can load, preventing XSS\n- **CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)**: Controls which domains can make API requests\n- **Secure cookies**: HttpOnly (prevents JS access), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite (prevents CSRF)\n- **Certificate pinning**: App only trusts specific certificates, preventing man-in-the-middle attacks\n\n### Security Headers\n\n```\nStrict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains\nContent-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'\nX-Content-Type-Options: nosniff\nX-Frame-Options: DENY\n```\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe the TLS handshake process\n- Explain the role of certificate authorities\n- List security headers and their purposes", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students inspect a website's SSL certificate in the browser (click the padlock icon). They note the CA, expiry date, and encryption algorithm. Then compare HTTP vs HTTPS sites using DevTools.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a diagram of the TLS handshake with numbered steps and blank labels for students to fill in.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think HTTPS makes a site 'safe'. Explain that HTTPS only encrypts the connection — the site itself could still be malicious (phishing sites can use HTTPS).\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'What happens if an SSL certificate expires? What will the user see and what should the site operator do?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Data transmission using the web", "content_text": "Investigate the effect of big data on web architecture", "explanation": "## Effect of Big Data on Web Architecture\n\nBig data — characterised by the 5 Vs (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) — has fundamentally changed how web systems are designed.\n\n### Architectural Changes Driven by Big Data\n\n- **Horizontal scaling**: Adding more servers instead of upgrading one. Distributed systems across multiple nodes.\n- **Microservices**: Breaking monolithic applications into independent services that can scale individually.\n- **Load balancing**: Distributing traffic across multiple servers (Nginx, AWS ALB) to handle high volume.\n- **CDN (Content Delivery Networks)**: Caching content at edge servers globally (Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront) to reduce latency.\n- **Database sharding**: Splitting databases across multiple servers by key ranges or hash values.\n- **NoSQL databases**: MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis for unstructured/semi-structured big data that doesn't fit relational schemas.\n- **Message queues**: Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ for asynchronous processing of high-volume data streams.\n- **Caching layers**: Redis, Memcached to reduce database load for frequently accessed data.\n\n### Big Data Processing Architectures\n\n- **Batch processing**: Hadoop MapReduce — process large datasets offline\n- **Stream processing**: Apache Kafka, Spark Streaming — process data in real-time\n- **Lambda architecture**: Combines batch and stream processing\n\n### Real-World Examples\n\n- Netflix processes 500 billion events per day using Kafka and microservices\n- Google indexes trillions of web pages using distributed systems\n- Social media platforms handle millions of concurrent users with sharded databases and CDNs\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain how big data requirements change web architecture\n- Describe specific technologies used for big data\n- Compare monolithic vs microservices architecture for big data", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Present a scenario — 'Your school website gets 100 visitors/day. Netflix gets 200 million users. How would the architecture differ?' Students design both systems on a whiteboard.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a comparison table: Small Scale Architecture vs Big Data Architecture, with rows for Database, Server, Caching, Processing.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think 'just get a bigger server' solves scaling. Explain vertical scaling limits and why horizontal scaling with distributed systems is necessary.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A streaming service experiences a 10x traffic spike during a popular show release. Name three architectural features that would handle this.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Investigate and explain the role of the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)", "explanation": "## The World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)\n\nW3C is the main international standards organisation for the World Wide Web, founded in 1994 by Tim Berners-Lee.\n\n### Role and Purpose\n\n- **Develops open web standards**: HTML, CSS, XML, SVG, WCAG, WAI-ARIA\n- **Ensures interoperability**: Standards ensure websites work consistently across browsers and devices\n- **Promotes accessibility**: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG 2.1) ensure web content is accessible to people with disabilities\n- **Future-proofing**: Standards evolve to accommodate new technologies while maintaining backward compatibility\n- **Open process**: Standards are developed through public review and consensus, not controlled by any single company\n\n### Key Standards\n\n| Standard | Purpose |\n|----------|--------|\n| HTML5 | Structure and content of web pages |\n| CSS3 | Presentation and styling |\n| WCAG 2.1 | Web accessibility guidelines (A, AA, AAA levels) |\n| XML | Data interchange format |\n| SVG | Scalable vector graphics for the web |\n| WAI-ARIA | Accessibility for dynamic web content |\n\n### Why W3C Matters for Developers\n\n- Valid HTML/CSS passes W3C validators, improving cross-browser compatibility\n- Following standards improves SEO (search engines prefer standards-compliant sites)\n- WCAG compliance may be legally required (e.g. government websites)\n- Standards reduce browser-specific hacks and workarounds\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe W3C's role in web development\n- Explain why web standards are important\n- Connect W3C accessibility standards to inclusive design", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students run their own HTML through the W3C Markup Validator (validator.w3.org) and fix the errors. Discuss why validation matters.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a valid and invalid HTML file. Students identify which follows W3C standards and why.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think W3C is a company that controls the internet. Clarify it's a standards body — it develops guidelines, not software or infrastructure.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Name two W3C standards and explain how each improves the web experience.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Model elements that form a web development system", "explanation": "## Elements of a Web Development System\n\nA web development system is a multi-tier architecture with distinct components working together.\n\n### Core Elements\n\n- **Client (Browser)**: Renders HTML/CSS, executes JavaScript, sends HTTP requests. The user interface layer.\n- **Web Server**: Receives HTTP requests and serves responses. Examples: Nginx, Apache, IIS.\n- **Application Server**: Executes business logic. Examples: Node.js (Express), Python (Django/Flask), PHP, Java (Spring Boot).\n- **Database Server**: Stores and retrieves persistent data. Relational (MySQL, PostgreSQL) or NoSQL (MongoDB, Redis).\n- **API Layer**: RESTful or GraphQL interfaces connecting front-end to back-end services.\n- **CDN (Content Delivery Network)**: Caches static assets at edge servers for faster delivery.\n- **Load Balancer**: Distributes traffic across multiple application servers.\n\n### Architecture Diagram\n\n```\nUser → Browser → CDN → Load Balancer → Web Server → App Server → Database\n ↕\n Cache (Redis)\n```\n\n### Communication Between Elements\n\n- Browser ↔ Server: HTTP/HTTPS requests and responses\n- App Server ↔ Database: SQL queries or ORM calls\n- Server ↔ External APIs: HTTP requests to third-party services\n- Real-time: WebSocket connections for live updates\n\n### Development vs Production Environments\n\n- **Development**: Local machine, localhost, hot reloading, debug mode\n- **Staging**: Production-like environment for testing before release\n- **Production**: Live environment serving real users, with monitoring and logging\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Draw and label a web system architecture diagram\n- Explain the role of each element\n- Describe how elements communicate", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students draw a web architecture diagram for a simple e-commerce site, labelling each component and the protocols used between them.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a partially completed diagram with some elements filled in. Students complete the missing components and describe data flow.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think the browser does everything. Emphasise the separation of client-side (what the user sees) and server-side (what processes the data).\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'When you add an item to your cart on an online store, what elements of the web system are involved? Trace the data flow.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Explore and explain the influence of a web browser on web development, including dev tools", "explanation": "## Browser Influence on Web Development\n\nBrowsers are the runtime environment for web applications, and their features and limitations directly shape development decisions.\n\n### Browser Rendering Engines\n\n- **Blink** (Chrome, Edge, Opera): Most widely used, sets de facto standards\n- **Gecko** (Firefox): Strong standards compliance, privacy focus\n- **WebKit** (Safari): iOS requirement — all iOS browsers use WebKit\n\n### Cross-Browser Challenges\n\n- Different CSS implementations (e.g. Safari vs Chrome flexbox behaviour)\n- JavaScript API support varies (check caniuse.com)\n- Polyfills bridge gaps for older browsers\n- CSS vendor prefixes (-webkit-, -moz-) for experimental features\n\n### Browser Developer Tools\n\n- **Elements panel**: Inspect and live-edit HTML/CSS. See box model, computed styles.\n- **Console**: Execute JavaScript, view errors and warnings, debug with console.log().\n- **Network tab**: Monitor all HTTP requests — URLs, methods, status codes, timing, payload sizes.\n- **Application tab**: Inspect localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, service workers, cache.\n- **Performance profiler**: Record and analyse rendering performance, identify bottlenecks.\n- **Lighthouse**: Automated audits for performance, accessibility, SEO, best practices.\n- **Sources/Debugger**: Set breakpoints, step through JavaScript code, inspect variables.\n\n### How Browsers Shape Development\n\n- Progressive enhancement: Build for basic browsers first, add features for modern ones\n- Responsive design: Browsers on different devices require flexible layouts\n- Security policies: Same-origin policy, CSP enforcement, cookie restrictions\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Describe DevTools panels and their uses\n- Explain cross-browser compatibility challenges\n- Discuss how browser security features affect development", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students open DevTools on a popular website and complete a scavenger hunt: find the page load time, count the number of HTTP requests, identify the largest file, find a JavaScript error in the console.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a guided worksheet with screenshots showing where to find each DevTools panel.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think DevTools are only for 'hacking'. Emphasise they are essential professional development tools used daily by web developers.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Name three DevTools panels and describe a debugging scenario where each would be useful.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Investigate cascading style sheets (CSS) and its impact on the design of a web application", "explanation": "## CSS and Its Impact on Web Design\n\nCSS (Cascading Style Sheets) separates presentation from content, enabling flexible, responsive, and maintainable web design.\n\n### Core CSS Concepts\n\n- **Selectors**: Target elements to style (element, class, ID, attribute, pseudo-class)\n- **Specificity**: Determines which styles apply when rules conflict (inline > ID > class > element)\n- **Box model**: Content → Padding → Border → Margin — controls element spacing\n- **Cascade**: Styles inherit and override based on specificity and source order\n\n### Modern CSS Layout\n\n- **Flexbox**: One-dimensional layout (row or column). Perfect for navigation bars, card layouts.\n- **Grid**: Two-dimensional layout (rows and columns). Perfect for page layouts, dashboards.\n- **Responsive design**: Media queries adapt layout to screen size.\n\n```css\n/* Responsive grid layout */\n.container {\n display: grid;\n grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(300px, 1fr));\n gap: 1rem;\n}\n\n@media (max-width: 768px) {\n .container {\n grid-template-columns: 1fr;\n }\n}\n```\n\n### Impact on Web Applications\n\n- **Separation of concerns**: HTML for structure, CSS for presentation, JS for behaviour\n- **Responsive design**: One codebase works across desktop, tablet, mobile\n- **Theming**: CSS custom properties (variables) enable dark mode and branding\n- **Performance**: External CSS files are cached, reducing page load times\n- **Accessibility**: CSS can enhance readability (font sizes, contrast, focus indicators)\n- **Animations**: CSS transitions and animations replace heavy JavaScript solutions\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain CSS specificity and the cascade\n- Describe how CSS enables responsive design\n- Compare Flexbox and Grid use cases", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students redesign a plain HTML page using only CSS — no HTML changes. They add layout, colours, typography, and responsive behaviour, demonstrating the power of CSS separation.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide the HTML structure and a CSS template with comments indicating where to add each style rule.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think CSS is 'just colours and fonts'. Show complex layouts, animations, and responsive designs achievable with CSS alone.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Explain why this CSS rule `.nav .link` has higher specificity than `.link` and what happens when both target the same element.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Investigate the reasons for version control and apply it when developing web application", "explanation": "## Version Control in Web Development\n\nVersion control systems (VCS) track changes to code over time, enabling collaboration, history tracking, and safe experimentation.\n\n### Why Version Control Is Essential\n\n- **Change tracking**: Every modification is recorded with who, when, and why\n- **Collaboration**: Multiple developers work on the same codebase without conflicts\n- **Branching**: Create isolated branches for features/experiments without affecting the main code\n- **Rollback**: Revert to any previous working state if bugs are introduced\n- **Code review**: Pull requests enable peer review before merging changes\n- **Accountability**: Audit trail of all changes for compliance and debugging\n\n### Git Workflow\n\n1. `git clone` — Copy repository locally\n2. `git branch feature/login` — Create a feature branch\n3. Make changes, `git add`, `git commit -m 'Add login form'`\n4. `git push origin feature/login` — Push to remote\n5. Create Pull Request — team reviews code\n6. Merge to main after approval\n7. `git tag v1.0.0` — Tag releases\n\n### Branching Strategies\n\n- **Git Flow**: main, develop, feature, release, hotfix branches\n- **GitHub Flow**: main + feature branches, deploy from main\n- **Trunk-based**: Short-lived branches merged to main frequently\n\n### Version Control for Web Applications\n\n- Track HTML, CSS, JS, configuration files\n- Use `.gitignore` to exclude `node_modules/`, `.env`, build artifacts\n- Integrate with CI/CD for automated testing and deployment on push\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain why version control is important in web development\n- Describe a typical Git workflow\n- Compare branching strategies", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students create a Git repository, make commits, create a branch, make conflicting changes, and resolve a merge conflict. This hands-on experience demonstrates the value of version control.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a step-by-step Git cheat sheet with commands and their purposes.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think version control means 'saving frequently' or keeping files like 'project_v2_final_FINAL.zip'. Show how Git provides a complete history without file duplication.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Your teammate's code broke the login page. Using Git, describe the steps to identify what changed and revert to the working version.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Explore the types and significance of code libraries for front-end web development", "explanation": "## Front-End Code Libraries\n\nCode libraries provide pre-written, tested, reusable code that accelerates development and enforces best practices.\n\n### Types of Front-End Libraries\n\n- **UI Component Libraries**: Bootstrap, Material UI, Tailwind CSS — provide pre-built buttons, forms, navigation, grids\n- **JavaScript Frameworks/Libraries**: React (UI components), Vue.js (progressive framework), Angular (full framework), Svelte (compiled)\n- **Utility Libraries**: Lodash (data manipulation), Axios (HTTP requests), Day.js (date handling)\n- **Animation Libraries**: GSAP, Framer Motion, Animate.css\n- **Chart/Data Visualisation**: Chart.js, D3.js, Plotly\n- **State Management**: Redux (React), Vuex/Pinia (Vue), NgRx (Angular)\n\n### Significance\n\n- **Development speed**: Reuse tested components instead of building from scratch\n- **Consistency**: Standardised components across the application\n- **Community support**: Bug fixes, security patches, documentation maintained by thousands of contributors\n- **Best practices**: Libraries encode patterns like component-based architecture, virtual DOM, reactive state\n- **Cross-browser compatibility**: Libraries handle browser differences internally\n\n### Considerations When Choosing Libraries\n\n- **Bundle size**: Large libraries increase page load time\n- **Maintenance**: Is the library actively maintained? Check last commit date, issue response time\n- **Licence**: MIT, Apache 2.0 allow commercial use; GPL has stricter requirements\n- **Security**: Check for known vulnerabilities (npm audit, Snyk)\n- **Learning curve**: Team's familiarity and documentation quality\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Name types of front-end libraries with examples\n- Explain the significance of code libraries in web development\n- Evaluate trade-offs of using third-party libraries", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students build the same UI component (a responsive card layout) twice — once from scratch with vanilla CSS, once using Bootstrap. Compare development time and result.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a comparison table: Library Name | Type | Purpose | Example Use | Licence.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think using a library means you're not a 'real developer'. Explain that professional developers use libraries extensively — the skill is in choosing and integrating them.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'A project uses 15 JavaScript libraries totalling 5MB. What problems might this cause and how would you address them?'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Explain the use and development of open-source software in relation to web development", "explanation": "## Open-Source Software in Web Development\n\nOpen-source software (OSS) has source code that is publicly available for anyone to inspect, modify, and distribute under its licence terms.\n\n### Open-Source Licences\n\n- **MIT**: Very permissive — use for anything, just include the licence\n- **Apache 2.0**: Like MIT but includes patent grants\n- **GPL (v2, v3)**: Copyleft — derivative works must also be open-source\n- **BSD**: Permissive, similar to MIT with slight variations\n\n### OSS in the Web Ecosystem\n\nThe majority of the web runs on open-source:\n- **Operating systems**: Linux powers most web servers\n- **Web servers**: Nginx, Apache\n- **Languages/runtimes**: Python, Node.js, PHP, Ruby\n- **Frameworks**: React, Vue.js, Django, Express, Laravel\n- **Databases**: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis\n- **Tools**: Git, Docker, Kubernetes, VS Code\n\n### Benefits of Open Source\n\n- **Free to use**: No licence costs for most OSS\n- **Transparency**: Code can be audited for security and quality\n- **Community**: Bugs are fixed faster with thousands of contributors\n- **Customisation**: Modify the code to fit specific needs\n- **Innovation**: Open collaboration drives rapid advancement\n\n### Challenges of Open Source\n\n- **Maintenance burden**: Many OSS projects depend on unpaid volunteers\n- **Security risks**: Popular libraries can be targeted (e.g. supply chain attacks via npm packages)\n- **Licence compliance**: GPL requirements can affect commercial products\n- **Quality variation**: Not all OSS is well-maintained or documented\n\n### Common Exam Question Angles\n\n- Explain OSS and its role in web development\n- Compare open-source licences\n- Discuss benefits and risks of using OSS in commercial projects", "teaching_example": "### How to teach this to students\n\n**Activity**: Students explore a popular open-source project on GitHub (e.g. React). They examine the licence, contributor count, issue tracker, and pull requests to understand how OSS development works.\n\n**Scaffolding**: Provide a structured research template: Project Name | Licence | Contributors | Stars | Last Updated | How It's Used in Web Dev.\n\n**Common Misconception**: Students think 'open-source' means 'no rules'. Emphasise that licences still govern how code can be used, especially GPL.\n\n**Quick Formative Assessment**: 'Can you use a GPL-licensed library in a commercial product? Explain the implications.'"}, {"subject": "SWE", "year": "Y12", "topic": "Programming for the web", "section": "Designing web applications", "content_text": "Investigate methods to support and manage the load times of web pages/applications", "explanation": "## Managing Web Page Load Times\n\nPage load speed directly impacts user experience, SEO rankings, and conversion rates. Google's Core Web Vitals are now ranking factors.\n\n### Key Performance Metrics\n\n- **LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)**: Time to render the largest visible element. Target: <2.5s\n- **FID (First Input Delay)**: Time from user interaction to browser response. Target: <100ms\n- **CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)**: Visual stability — elements shouldn't jump around. Target: <0.1\n\n### Optimisation Methods\n\n**1. Image Optimisation**\n- Use modern formats (WebP, AVIF) — 30-50% smaller than JPEG\n- Responsive images with `srcset` attribute\n- Lazy loading: ``\n- Compress images (TinyPNG, Squoosh)\n\n**2. Code Optimisation**\n- Minification: Remove whitespace, comments from CSS/JS\n- Code splitting: Load only the JavaScript needed for the current page\n- Tree shaking: Remove unused code from bundles\n- Defer non-critical JS: `
